column3: To fabricate membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs), 0.1 g of oxide catalyst was added to 0.5 g of Hispec 1860, a commercial platinum-on-carbon catalyst. Aqueous Nafion solution (11.92 wt % solids, 1 mL) was added to produce an ink containing 110 wt % Nafion with respect to oxide catalyst. The ink was then shear-mixed in a planetary mixer using 5 mm diameter yttrium stabilized zirconia beads for 3 min at 3000 rpm. The ink was manually stirred to break apart any sediment at which point it was mixed for a further 5 min in the planetary mixer. This ink was also used for wet cell testing. The ink was screen printed onto a PTFE sheet to give a layer with an overall platinum group metal loading between 0.05 and 0.15 mg cm–2, verified using X-ray fluorescence measurements. The layer was then transferred from the PTFE sheet onto Nafion N117, using a hot press. A Pt/C layer was simultaneously transferred to the opposite side of the Nafion N117 membrane, to produce the catalyst coated membrane. For testing, the catalyst coated membrane was placed between two flow field plates backed with microporous layer coated carbon paper, Toray TGP-H-060. Testing was carried out at 80 °C with 10 psig of humidified H2/N2 gas. The electrochemical surface areas of the platinum in both sides of the catalyst coated membrane were measured with CO stripping voltammetry. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were collected, scanning the potential from 0.02 to 1.6 V vs reversible hydrogen electrode at 5 mV s–1. The membrane electrode assembly was then aged by cycling between 0.05 and 1.0 V 500 times at 100 mV s–1. After aging, a final cyclic voltammogram was collected, scanning the potential from 0.02 to 1.6 V at 5 mV s–1.

For liquid cell electrochemical testing, inks were spray coated onto a Toray paper (hydrophobic gas diffusion layer 60) at 0.2 mg cm–2 loading, verified using X-ray fluorescence (XRF) measurements. The buttons were then soaked overnight in an equivalent solution as the test solution (1 M H2SO4 (VWR AVS TITRINORM)) under a vacuum to allow ingress of solution into the gas diffusion layer, ensuring all of the catalyst is in contact with electrolyte. A button was then placed in an electrochemical cell that contained 1 M H2SO4 while being purged with nitrogen (liquid nitrogen off gas) and held at 60 °C. A reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) (hydrogen bubbled over Pt/C catalyst) and Pt wire was used as a reference and counter electrode, respectively. First, the cell was cycled between 0 and 1.35 V vs RHE at different scan rates (5–300 mV s–1), and then, an activity sweep was performed between 1 and 1.55 V vs RHE at 1 mV s–1. Then, a degradation cycle between 0.6 and 1.35 V vs RHE at 100 mV s–1 for 1000 cycles was performed (∼4 h 10 min). This was chosen as the potential is brought up to the onset of OER, while not forming bubbles, which could affect the results. An end of life activity test was then performed after the degradation cycles equivalent to the start of life (CVs at different scan rates and then an activity sweep). The activity data were iR corrected by taking the high frequency intercept of an impedance scan measured at 1.45 V vs RHE; typical values were between 0.2 and 0.35 Ω. Activity data were normalized to a mass activity by measuring the loading of the ruthenium by XRF and then assuming the ratio of the EDXA results. Degradation was monitored by taking a 1 mL sample of the electrolyte solution at the start of testing and then after the beginning of life (BOL) activity test, degradation test, and end of life (EOL) activity test for inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis. The 1 mL sample was diluted with water to 1 v.% H2SO4, and then, 1 v.% HCl was added. These samples were injected directly into an ICP-MS (Agilent Technologies 7700 series) to obtain the concentration of metal leached into solution.

The electrochemical performance of the catalysts for OER and HER in 1 M KOH was evaluated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and galvanostatic measurements on an Autolab type III potentiostat/galvanostat in the standard three electrode configuration. Following the published best practice of excluding any presence of PGM in the evaluation of non-PGM catalysts,  the three-electrode cell contained a catalyst-loaded NF working electrode, a graphite rod counter electrode, and a 3 M KCl Ag/AgCl reference electrode with a 3 M KCl salt bridge. All measured potentials were converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale at pH 14 (E = +1.0374 V with respect to the 3 M KCl Ag/AgCl reference electrode). The catalyst activity and stability in OER and HER were measured in a deaerated 1 M KOH solution (pH 14) at 25 °C (under flowing N2 after 30 min of N2 purging). All linear sweep voltammograms were based on the same catalyst loading and scan rate (5 mV s–1) and iR-corrected. The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) of the catalysts were estimated by their double layer capacitance in the non-Faradaic region using scan rates from 5 to 50 mV s–1. The OER and HER activities of 20% Pt/C and IrO2 on NF were also measured for performance benchmarking. The working electrodes for the latter were prepared by ultrasonicating 8 mg Pt/C (or IrO2 powder) and 1 mL polytetrafluoroethylene dispersion in ethanol for 30 min, followed by drop-casting the catalyst ink onto a NF (1 cm × 1 cm) foam and air-drying at room temperature. The catalyst loading was kept at ∼5.0 mg cm–2. Electrolytic cells for water splitting were assembled by using two identical catalyst-loaded nickel foams as the anode and the cathode in 1 M KOH solution. The oxygen and hydrogen gases released were collected and measured to calculate the Faradaic efficiency.

The electrocatalytic activities were carried out using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation with the three-electrode cell, where a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a graphite rod were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The Ni3S2/NF (1 cm × 1 cm) was directly used as the working electrode. For the Pt/C/NF and RuO2/NF, 4 mg of 20% Pt/C or RuO2 was dissolved in 1 mL of a mixture of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone and H2O (volume ratio is 1:1), and 20 μL of 5% polyvinylidene difluoride solution was added to the mixture with ultrasonic treatment to obtain the homogenous ink. Then, 0.07 mL of ink was loaded onto a piece of 1 cm2 NF 10 times by the drop-coating method (loading mass: 2.8 mg cm–2). KOH (1.0 M) electrolyte was used for the electrochemical tests. All of the potential values versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) were calculated by the equation: E(RHE) = E(SCE) + 0.0591pH + 0.2415 V. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were collected at a scan rate of 2 mV s–1. A 90% iR-correction was applied for all LSV curves. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out with various scan rates ranging from 10 to 200 mV s–1 to estimate the electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs). The CV potential window for HER is 0.1–0.2 V versus RHE, and for OER, it is 1.05–1.15 versus RHE. Amperometric curves and chronopotentiometric tests were conducted to explore the stability of the Ni3S2/NF samples. The multipotential step (from 100 to 800 mV vs RHE) and multicurrent step (from 10 to 100 mA cm–2) were also measured for the HER and OER processes of the Ni3S2/NF samples, respectively. The faradaic efficiency of overall water splitting of Ni3S2/NF-4 was estimated by measuring the experimentally generated gas versus the theoretically calculated value at 1.65 V for 120 min. The amounts of gas were determined by gas chromatography (Fuli, 9790II) connected with a two-electrode cell.

The electrochemical tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (ChenHua CHI 660D). The measurements for the OER and HER were carried out in a standard three-electrode system at room temperature using 1 M KOH (pH = 14) solution as the electrolyte. A piece of nickel foam (1 cm × 1 cm) sandwiched on a platinum electrode was used as the working electrode, while a graphite rod was employed as the counter electrode and Hg/HgO as the reference electrode. The preparation of the working electrode is displayed in Figure S1 (Supporting Information). Overall water splitting was studied in a two-electrode system utilizing two platinum electrodes sandwiched by a piece of nickel foam (1 cm × 1 cm). For comparison, commercial RuO2@NF (mass loading is 0.68 mg cm–2) and Pt/C(20%)@NF (mass loading is 0.72 mg cm–2) were used as catalysts for the OER and HER, respectively. All of the measured potentials were transformed to reversible hydrogen electrodes (RHE) on the basis of the formula: E(vs RHE) = E(vs Hg/HgO) + 0.098 V + 0.0591 × pH. The scan rate of linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves was set at 1 mV s–1, and the Tafel slopes were calculated from relevant polarization curves at a strongly polarized region. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were tested in the frequency range of 100 000–0.01 Hz at a given overpotential.

The stability of materials was determined by chronopotentiometry measurements for 80 h at a constant current density of 200 mA cm–2 for the OER and −10 mA cm–2 for the HER, respectively. In addition, chronoamperometry measurements were adopted to characterize the stability at a constant overpotential for the OER and HER. The Cdl values were obtained by cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves with different scan rates from 20 to 100 mV s–1 in the potential range of 1.123–1.223 V versus RHE. All of the above measurements were calibrated with 85% iR compensation except for CV. The calculation of the turnover frequency (TOF) is shown below. The values of TOF are calculated according to eq 1 assuming that all Ni and V ions in the catalysts are active and contribute to the catalytic reaction. (1)Here, j is the measured current density (mA cm–2), A is the area of the electrode, F is the Faraday constant (96485.3 C mol–1), and m is the number of moles of catalysts that are loaded on nickel foam, which is calculated from inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) results. All of the electrochemical measurements were carried out in air. Electrochemical surface areas (ECSAs) are calculated based on eq 2.

The electrochemical tests were obtained using a PARSTAT MC-1000 electrochemical workstation. The prepared NiO@NiMoO4 nanosheets with 1.4 mg cm–2, NiO nanosheets with 0.3 mg cm–2, and NiMoO4 with 1.0 mg cm–2 on the nickel foam were directly used as electrodes. The Hg/HgO electrode and platinum sheet were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. OER tests were performed in an O2–-purged aqueous solution of 1 M KOH in polypropylene beakers. All linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were tested at a scanning rate of 5 mV s–1. All polarization potentials reported here are iR-compensated and converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), and current densities are per geometric area. Chronopotentiometry was measured at 10 mA cm–2 for 12 h. The uncompensated solution resistance (Ru) was determined between the frequency range 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with a 5 mV amplitude.

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a three-electrode system connected to an Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation at room temperature. A rotating disk working electrode (WE) made of glassy carbon (GC, PINE, 5 mm diameter, 0.196 cm2) was used. Catalyst powder (5 mg) and carbon powder (Vulcan XC72, 5 mg) were dispersed in a mixture of water (750 μL), ethanol (250 μL), and Nafion solution (40 μL) under continuous ultrasonication. Subsequently, 8 μL of the catalyst ink was transferred to a polished GC electrode and allowed to dry naturally. A Hg/HgO electrode in 1 M KOH aqueous solution was used as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire counter electrode was placed in a fritted glass tube. The electrolyte was an aqueous solution of 1 M KOH, saturated with oxygen, and bubbled for 30 min before each test. OER polarization curves were obtained at a scan rate of 5 mV s–1 in O2-saturated 1 M KOH solutions. All potential versus RHE is in line with the equation E(RHE) = E(Hg/HgO) + 0.059pH + 0.096 V. The pH of the 1 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature was 13.60.

The electrocatalytic properties were evaluated in a typical three-electrode setup at ambient temperature using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation, in which the samples were used directly as working electrodes (geometric area: 0.25 cm2), and a high-purity graphite rod and an Hg/HgO electrode were the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For a more accurate comparison, the MOF powders were examined for reference. The catalyst suspension was prepared by ultrasonically assisted dispersion of 5 mg of the MOF in 1 mL of a solution containing 0.96 mL of isopropyl alcohol and 40 μL of a 5 wt % Nafion solution. Then 250 μL of the above suspension was dropped on the cleaned Ni foam electrode (0.25 cm2) as the working electrode. All of the potentials were referenced to the RHE, according to ERHE = EHg/HgO + 0.098 + 0.059 × pH. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (95% IR compensation), cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), chronopotentiometry, and multicurrent step curves were recorded in 1 M KOH. The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) were determined via the CV method.

The electrocatalytic performance of the prepared material was evaluated using a three-electrode system. The three-electrode system consisted of a working electrode, a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and a counter electrode (graphite rod). The synthesized material was coated on a Ni foam substrate. Before that, Ni foam was treated with HCl (1 M), and the acid was further washed by sonicating it with distilled water and was finally sonicated with ethanol for 15 min and dried in an oven for 6 h. The electrochemical performance was studied by mixing the active materials (80 wt %), carbon black (10 wt %), and PVDF (10 wt %) as the binder. They were homogenously mixed in NMP, and then, the slurry was coated on nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2). It was then dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. The mass of the dried foam was noted and taken for OER studies. The mass loading of the catalyst was found to be 2.2 mg/cm2.

Reactive ion etching was performed in an IONVAC inductively coupled plasma (ICP) reactor (PGF 600 RF HUTTER). The FNC samples were placed in the chamber, followed by evacuation to 30 mTorr. O2 gas was introduced at a flow rate of 20 sccm, and the glow discharge was ignited at 50 W on both sides of the NC (5 min) and at 25 W only on the CNT-F side (15 min). Alternatively, the CNT surface of the FNC electrode was chemically etched by means of a piranha solution for 2 h (30 mL of H2O2 with 70 mL of a concentrated H2SO4 solution).  We will refer to the electrodes treated with RIE as FNC-RE and those treated with piranha solution as FNC-WE. After etching treatments, the back of the electrodes was covered with silver paste, dried under an infrared (IR) lamp for 60 min, and covered with paraffin. In this way, we create an effective back-contact to collect the charges, while preventing the direct contact between silver and the electrolyte solution.

Three milligrams of each of the above sample powders (i.e., catalyst) was first dispersed in 0.9 mL of a mixed solution of water and ethanol (2:1, v/v), and then 45 μL of Nafion solution (5 wt %) was added. The catalyst suspension was ultrasonically treated for 30 min in order to obtain a homogeneous ink. To prepare the working electrode, 4.2 μL of the mixture ink was dripped onto a polished glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3 mm (catalyst loading 0.2 mg·cm–2) then dried at room temperature for 12 h.

Electrochemical operando ATR-FTIR spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker 70 V FTIR spectrometer with an attenuated-toal-reflection (ATR) configuration using a liquid nitrogen cooled MCT detector. Based on a specially-made three-electrode thin-layer FTIR cell configuration, a FITR radiation sequentially passed by the CaF2 window and thin-layer solutions, and then it was reflected by the electrode surfaces. Before each FTIR data collection, the 0.1 M KOH electrolyte was saturated with O2 gas, and several fast cyclic voltammograms (500 mV s−1) between 0.05 and 1.35 V versus RHE were first taken to obtain a stable current response. The FTIR spectra were collected at an open-circuit voltage, and a potential range of 1.0-1.6 V versus RHE with an interval of 0.1 V. Each infrared absorption spectrum was acquired at a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The electrochemical tests were examined in a typical three-electrode system, using a carbon rod as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. All potential was converted to the RHE scale based on formula of ERHE = ESCE + 0.244 + 0.059 × pH. A typical preparation process of working electrodes was dispersing 10 mg catalysts in 500 μl DI water, 450 μl isopropyl alcohol and 50 μl Nafion (wt 5%) by ultrasonication for 35 min. Then 100 μL ink was spread on cleaned NF (1 cm × 1 cm) surface with a loading of 2–3 mg/cm2. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) towards HER and OER were measured at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH (pH = 14). After that, potentials and voltages calibrate by iR corrected. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained with 0.01-105 HZ frequency. Double layer capacitance (Cdl) values were obtained by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at the different scan rates 20–100 mV s−1. The long-term durability assay was tested by chronopotentiometry measurements. The overall water splitting was performed on a two-electrode system, and 1 T/2H MoSe2/MXene electrodes were served as both cathode and anode.

The basic electrode of GCE was mechanically polished by 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm alumina slurry in turn to obtain a mirror-like surface, and then washed with ethanol and DDW with the assistance of sonication to remove the contaminants adsorbed during polishing. Then the electrode was blow-dried with N2 for use. The catalytic ink was prepared by the following procedure: 2.0 mg of synthesised ZnCo-LDH NS catalyst was dispersed in 0.5 mL of ethanol and DDW mixture (Vethanol: VDDW = 4:1), followed by the addition of 10 µL of 5.0 wt% Nafion solution. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous ink. After that, 5.0 µL of the catalyst ink was dropped onto the cleaned GCE and dried under room temperature. After gently washed with DDW, the ZnCo-LDH modified electrode was achieved. The loading amount (m1) of the catalyst was calculated to be 0.28 mg cm−2 according to the geometrical area (0.07 cm−2) of GCE. The Zn-OH, Co-OH and RuO2 modified electrodes were prepared in the same manner.

All of the electrochemical measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CS350, CorrTest, China) with a standard three-electrode system in a rotating disk electrode (RDE) configuration. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 5 mm in diameter) modified with catalysts was used as the working electrode. Typically, 4 mg catalyst and 30 μL 5 wt% Nafion solution were dispersed in 1 mL mixture solution of Milli-Q water (18 MΩ cm−1) and absolute ethanol (1:4, v/v) by at least 30 min sonication to form a homogeneous ink. Then the above catalyst inks were dropcasted onto the surface of the glassy carbon electrode with the loading of ~ 0.285 mg cm-2 and dried at room temperature for further measurement. Graphite rod and Ag/AgCl electrode in saturated KCl solution were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. 1.0 M KOH with saturated O2 was applied as the electrolyte. The cyclic voltammetry curves were recorded with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The LSV polarization curves were conducted at a rate of 5 mV/s in O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH solution without the ohmic potential drop (iR) correction. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are carried out in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. The chronopotentiometry was carried out under a constant current density of 10 mA cm-2 in O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH solution with the working electrode continuously rotating at 1600 rpm to remove the oxygen bubbles. The durability test was performed by cyclic voltammetry for 3000 circles, then the LSV polarization curves before and after CV were recorded for comparison. The Cdl was calculated by performing CV measurements at different scan rates of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 mV/s in the potential range of 1.20–1.30 V (vs RHE). The value of Cdl was a half of the linear slope obtained from plotting Δj = ja-jc at 1.25 V against the scan rates (ja and jc are anode and cathode current density, respectively). All the potentials were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (vs RHE), and the corresponding equation is ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 1.018 V (Fig. S1).

Ink of CoFe2O4, NiFe2O4 and RuO2 samples were prepared in 300 μL of Isopropanol by dispersing 5 mg sample. 30 μL of Nafion was added as adhesive and after that the whole mixture was sonicated to have an even dispersion for 30 min. 5.0 μL ink was deposited on glassy carbon electrode which is of 3 mm diameter which gives rise catalyst loading 1.06 mg/cm2.

Electrochemical impedance measurement for CoFe2O4, NiFe2O4 and the physical mixture of both, was performed in a three electrode system. To measure the impedance for the synthesized samples onset potential was chosen as the execution bias of the respective materials. The impedance measurement was carried out keeping the sweeping of frequency from 50 KHz to 1 Hz with a 10 mV AC dither.

In order to evaluate the electrocatalytic oxygen evolution performance of these obtained samples, electrochemistry measurement was carried out on CHI 760 E instrument. The standard three-electrode system was used for this electrochemical test at room temperature in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte saturated with O2. In 1.0 M KOH solution, carbon rod is used as the counter electrode, Hg/HgO is used as the reference electrode and the glassy carbon electrode supported catalyst is served as the working electrode. The linear sweep voltammetry test (LSV) was conducted in a scanning rate of 5 mV·s−1 in the range of 0.924 V–1.724 V (vs. RHE). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was accomplished in the range of 1 Hz–100 kHz. Cyclic voltammetry tests (CV) were performed in the range of 1.024–1.124 V (vs. RHE) at sweep rates of 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160 and 200 mV s−1 respectively, which is used to calculate the Cdl value. A chronoamperometry (i-t) was handled to measure the stability of the catalyst in 1 M KOH solution. According to the Nernst equation, the electrode potential was converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential with respect to the Hg/HgO electrode: E (vs RHE) = E (vs Hg/HgO) + 0.097 V + 0.0591 pH.

Electrochemical tests were taken place on an electrochemical working station (CHI760E, Shanghai) in 1.0 M KOH solution. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were employed to evaluate the supercapacitive performance, where the as-prepared samples acted as a working electrode and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and Pt wire electrode worked as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The corresponding long-term stability was tested on LAND battery test system (CT2001A, Wuhan). The electrocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples towards HER and OER were assessed by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). The Pt wire was replaced by a graphite rod as the counter electrode. The i-t curve was also measured for 20 h to examine the stability of the electrode.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua) using a typical three-electrode system with an O2-purged 1.0 M KOH electrolyte solution. A Pt wire counters electrode (CE), an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) and NiFe-LDH/NiCo2O4/NF working electrode (WE) were employed in this mode. The loading amount of nanocomposites on the Ni foam is about 3.5 mg cm−2. After IR compensation, the cyclic voltammogram (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of all samples were tested to evacuate OER performance. All LSV curves involved in the figure were tested after the 60 cycles CV test. All measured potentials by Ag/AgCl electrode were converted into standard reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) based on the equation of ERHE = EAg/AgCl (3 M) +0.059 pH + 0.197. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was estimated by measuring current density associated with electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) from scan rate dependent of a series of CV curves. Specifically, the voltammograms were used in a non-faradic region between 0.15 V and 0.25 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and the current density values corresponding to 0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) were obtained by sweeping CV curves at various scan rates of 20 mV/s, 40 mV/s, 60 mV/s, 80 mV/s, and 100 mV/s respectively. By fitting those data to a line, the slope of the line corresponds to 2Cdl. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded in the above-mentioned three-electrode battery with a frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.1 Hz in 1.0 M KOH solution. The stability of catalysts was studied by chronoamperometry at a certain potential of 1.590 V for 24 h.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were performed at room temperature in 1.0 M KOH solution on a three-electrode setup system i.e., CHI 760E (Chenhua, China). Before each experiment, CHI software was used for iR compensation. A modified-glassy carbon electrode (modified-GCE) (5 mm, 0.197 cm2) was used as the working electrode mounted on a rotating apparatus. To prepare modified-GCE, a catalyst loading of 0.25 mg cm−2 was made on GCE via drop-casting catalyst ink suspension i.e., 2 mg catalytic material into ethanol (1 mL) with 5% Nafion (10 μL).

All electrochemical measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) with a three-electrode setup. Hg/HgO electrode was used as the reference electrode. Pt electrode and a graphite rod were used as the counter electrode in OER and HER measurements, respectively. Glassy carbon electrode coated with samples was used as the working electrode. The preparation process of the working electrode was as follows: The sample (2.5 mg) was dispersed in ethanol (0.5 mL) and sonicated for 1 h. 5 wt% Nafion D-520 dispersion (25 μL) was then added in the above suspension, followed by sonication for another 30 min. The suspension (5 μL) was then deposited on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode and dried overnight. Before the electrochemical measurements, the electrolyte (1 M KOH) was purged with argon for 30 min to remove the dissolved gas. In all measurements, the potentials were calculated with respect to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) based on the following equation: E(RHE) = E(SCE) + 0.059×pH + 0.244 V. All the potentials are referred to RHE without specification. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out with a scan rate of 2 mV s−1. EIS analysis was conducted in the frequency range of 10 mHz–100 kHz with 5 mV AC amplitude at an overpotential of 100 mV in 1.0 M KOH.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode system at an electrochemical workstation (PalmSens3), where a Pt wire and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) served as counter and reference electrodes. The OER performance was studied by using a rotating disk electrode (10 mm diameter, 0.785 cm2). The glassy carbon electrode was first cleaned and polished to a mirror finish with 50 nm Al2O3. In all, 32 μL of dispersion was transferred onto the glassy carbon disk and then dried at room temperature. The dispersion was prepared by dispersing 5 mg of nanoparticle powder in 1 mL ultrapure water, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. The LSV curves were recorded with a scan rate of 10 mV/s in 1.0 M KOH solution from 0 V to 0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. The CV measurements were performed at a scan rate of 50 mV/s from 0 V to 0.65 V (vs Ag/AgCl). Electrochemical data normalised to electrode geometric surface area was provided in Supplementary Fig. 5. Another set of electrochemical measurements was carried out at the same conditions except for the KOD solution in D2O for APT specimen preparation and measurements. The deuterium oxide (D2O with 99.9 at.% D) and potassium deuteroxide solution (40 wt.% KOD in D2O with 98 at.% D) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. EIS was performed under OER conditions by applying a sine wave signal with a 10 mV amplitude in the frequency range from 6 kHz to 0.2 Hz after equilibrating 5 s at 0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl for Co2FeO4 and 0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl for CoFe2O4. The software RelaxIS 3 (rhd instruments) was used for data treatment and analysis (details were given in Supplementary Note 4, Supplementary Figs. 24 and 25).

Before preparing the needle-shaped APT specimen, a bulk material containing nanoparticles was fabricated by the following procedure. A nanoparticle suspension was prepared by mixing 5.0 mg of nanoparticles in 1.0 ml of D2O water, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. 20 μL of nanoparticle solutions were dropped on a clean glassy carbon and dried at room temperature overnight. The CV measurements were performed on the glassy carbon electrodes in 1 M KOD (in D2O) solution at a scan rate of 50 mV/s from 0 V to 0.65 V (vs Ag/AgCl) for 100, 500 and 1000 cycles, respectively. Afterwards, the glassy carbon electrode was covered by Ni electrodeposition at a constant voltage of −1.5 V for 300 s in an electrolyte mixed with 3.0 g nickel sulphate, 0.5 g nickel chloride, and 0.5 g boric acid in 10 ml DI water. This bulk material was subsequently used to prepare needle-shaped APT specimens by a lift-out procedure using a focus ion beam/scanning electron microscope (FEI Helios G4 CX) (details are shown in Supplementary Note 2 and Supplementary Fig. 11). The APT experiments were conducted in CAMECA LEAP 5000XR instrument in laser pulsing mode at a specimen temperature of 57 K, laser energy of 30 pJ, pulse frequency of 125 kHz, and detection rates of 0.5. The APT data are reconstructed and analysed using the commercial IVAS 3.8.2 software. Additional APT data and analysis were listed in Supplementary Figs. 12–22 and Supplementary Note 3.

The H2 TPR measurements were conducted in a flow set-up consisting of a gas supply, a stainless-steel U-tube reactor heated in a ceramic tube furnace, and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD, Hydros 100). For the measurement, 116.6 mg catalyst nanoparticles were pre-treated in 50 Nml min−1 He (99.9999%) at 400 °C for 1 h. After cooling to 60 °C, the set-up was flushed with 50 Nml min−1 4.58% H2 (99.9999%)/Ar (99.9995%) for 1 h. The furnace was heated to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 K min−1. The maximum temperature was kept constant for 1 h. The temperature of the sample was measured every two seconds by a thermocouple placed inside the reactor. The arising water was condensed in a cold trap. The measured consumption of H2 and temperature were plotted against the measurement time. Experimental data was shown in Supplementary Fig. 23 and Supplementary Note 3.

Figure 1 illustrates the fabrication process of the 3D-printed pyramid electrode with a NiFe-LDH electrocatalyst for OER. The electrode, comprising pyramid arrays, was prepared via a sequential process including ink-based 3D printing and electrodeposition, i.e., (i) direct-ink-writing of graphene 3D pyramid array and electrodeposition of (ii) Cu (conductive layer) and (iii) NiFe-LDH (electrocatalyst layer) on the printed pyramid array. Graphene 3D pyramids were printed onto a flat square Cu foil (base area = 0.6 × 0.6 cm2) with successive movements of the nozzle (inner diameter, ID = 100 μm) filled with a properly designed graphene ink (30 wt. % graphene microflakes, GMFs, and the 70 wt. % polymer solution). Each pyramid had a bottom area of 500 × 500 μm2, height (h) of 600 μm, and inclination angle of 67.4° (Movie S1). To evaluate the adhesion of the printed graphene to Cu foil, a simple tape test was conducted. The printed graphene pattern provides good adhesion to Cu foil (Fig. S1). This design enables a surface area twice larger than a flat electrode, thereby increasing the number of active sites on the electrode. For efficient deposition of the NiFe-LDH electrocatalyst on 3D electrodes, the Cu conductive layer was formed on the printed graphene pyramids through electrodeposition conducted at an applied potential of −0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in the electrolyte (0.5 M CuSO4 5H2O) for 30 min (deposition temperature: 25 °C). Further electrodeposition of NiFe was performed to produce the NiFe-LDH electrocatalyst on the pyramids with the deposited Cu layer at an applied potential of − 1.3 V (vs. saturated calomel electrode, SCE) in the electrolyte (3 mM Ni(NO3)2 6H2O and 3 mM Fe(NO3)3 9H2O) for 15 min (deposition temperature: 10 °C). The deposition of Cu and NiFe was confirmed by the changing color of the 3D electrode shown in the optical images in Fig. 1.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were carried out on a CHI 660e potentiostat. All the electrochemical characterizations were carried out at the temperature of 298 K, which was controlled by thermostatic water-bath. OER were studied in a standard three-electrode glass cell connected to CHI 660e workstation using the prepared materials as the working electrode, a Pt mesh as the counter electrode and a Hg/HgO electrode as the reference electrode. All the measured potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrodes (RHE) according to ERHE = EHg/HgO +  0.059 pH + 0.0977 V. The LSV measurements were performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 to evaluate the catalytic activities of the electrode films. All the polarization measurements were iR compensated (80%) unless otherwise stated. Chronopotentiometry measurements were recorded under the same experimental setup without iR correction.

Ten milligrams of catalyst powder was dispersed in 300 μL of isopropyl alcohol and ultrasonically mixed for ~30 min. One microliter of the obtained catalyst slurry was dropped on a polished GC electrode, and then, 2 μL of Nafion (5 wt% in ethanol, Alfa Aesar) was cast onto the above coating and dried in air at room temperature (this yielded an approximate metal loading of 0.5 mg cm−2).

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a computer-controlled electrochemical analyzer (Supplementary Fig. 32). Catalyst-modified carbon paper was used as the WE, Pt wire as the counter electrode (CE) and Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode (RE). In situ XAFS was used to probe the valence state and coordination environment for amorphous Ir NSs during OER process. The dilute catalyst slurry (50 μl ink) was evently distributed on the carbon paper (3 cm × 3 cm).

The glassy carbon electrode (GC) (Φ = 3 mm, A: 0.0706 cm2) was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powders, and rinsed with acetone, ethanol, and DI water. GC was calibrated in 1.0 mM K3Fe(CN)6 + 1.0 mM K4Fe(CN)6 + 0.1 M KCl (see CV in Supplementary Fig. 1). The catalyst ink contained 3 mg catalyst, 50 μL 5 wt% Nafion solution, 50 μL DI water and 900 μL ethanol. After sonication for 2 h, 8 μL of catalyst ink was drop-casted on the polished GC (mass loading 0.340 mg cm-2) and dried in air.

FeP–CoP/NC was brush-painted on the cleaned carbon cloth (2 × 2 cm), the mass loading of FeP–CoP/NC was 3.17 mg cm−2. The obtained FeP–CoP/NC/CC was dried in a vacuum oven overnight to remove all the organic solvents.

1.32 g Ni(OH)2, 0.2493 g CoO, 0.0665 g polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), and 0.016625 g carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) were mixed with water to form the slurry. The slurry was sequentially brush painted on a piece of cleaned Ni foam (2 × 2 cm), dried and pressed under a pressure of 20 MPa. The mass loading of Ni(OH)2 was 330 mg cm-2. To prevent the delamination of the coated film under redox cycle in reaction, this coated Ni piece was then sandwiched by two pristine Ni foam piece, as shown in Supplementary Fig. 2, and pressed again under a pressure of 20 MPa to form the final AE.

Electrochemical characterizations including cyclic voltammetry (CV), LSV and chronopotentiometry were carried out on a Gamry Reference 3000 electrochemical instrument using a three-electrode electrochemical system. A 1 M KOH solution was used as electrolyte, and an Ag/AgCl electrode with saturated KCl filling solution and Pt wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. For catalyst grown on nickel foam (NiSe2, NixFe1−xSe2 and NiFe-LDH), they were used as work electrodes directly. Hot glue was employed to fix the working area at 0.2 cm−2 (0.5 × 0.4 cm). Before test, the reference electrode was measured against another unused Ag/AgCl reference electrode stored in saturated KCl solution. Calibration of Ag/AgCl reference electrodes was done by measuring the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential using a Pt electrode under a H2 atmosphere. During the test, Ag/AgCl reference electrode was constructed to a double-junction electrode to minimize contact between KOH and KCl. CVs were performed at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1, and the average of the two potentials at which the current crossed zero was taken to be the thermodynamic potential for the hydrogen electrode reaction, In 1 M KOH electrolytes, Evs.RHE=Evs.Ag/AgCl+1.009 V, and overpotential for OER was η=Evs.RHE−1.23 V=Evs.Ag/AgCl−0.221 V. Ohmic drop correction was performed using the current interrupt method by the potentiostat. Before recording the catalytic activity, catalysts were activated by a chronopotentiometry scan with constant current density of 5–10 mA cm−2 until reaching a stable state.

Normally, we set the pretreatment time at 12 h. Following the preconditioning, two cycles of LSVs were measured at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. Tafel slopes were calculated based on the LSV curves by plotting overpotential against log(current density). Chronopotentiometry measurements were performed to evaluate the long-term stability. The ECSA was determined by measuring the capacitive current associated with double-layer charging from the scan-rate dependence of CVs. For this, the potential window of CVs was 0.2–0.3 V versus Ag/AgCl. The scan rates were 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1,000 mV s−1. The double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was estimated by plotting the ΔJ=(Ja−Jc) at 0.25 V versus Ag/AgCl against the scan rate. The linear slope is twice of the double-layer capacitance Cdl. The measurements of O2 were performed using an Ocean Optics Multifrequency Phase Fluorimeter (MFPF-100) with a FOXY-OR 125 probe (see in Supplementary Methods).

In situ Raman spectra were acquired under controlled potentials using a homemade cell, which consisted of a working electrode (electrodeposit on 3 mm graphite) at the top, a Pt wire counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) reference electrode. Raman spectra were collected using a confocal microscope (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 633 nm, a power of 1–3 mW, and a spectral resolution of ∼1 cm−1. The spot size of the laser beam was estimated to be 1–2 μm. Acquisition time was typically 20 s for the spectral window of 1100 cm−1. Spectral shifts were calibrated routinely against the value of a silicon wafer (520.7 cm−1). The working electrode was anodically scanned from open circuit potential to 1.6 V at a rate of 0.2 mV s−1. Operando XRD measurements were conducted on Rigaku diffractometer at room temperature using the same cell. Diffraction data were collected with speed of 20° min−1 at 2θ range of 10–43°.

The as-prepared NF samples were directly used as the working electrode without any other current collector. All electrochemical tests were carried out in a three-electrode testing system (CHI 660D Electrochemical Workstation, Chenhua, Shanghai), with a KOH solution (1 M, pH = 13.6) as the electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed using a platinum foil as the auxiliary electrode (for HER measurement, carbon rod was used as the counter electrode) and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The electrolyte was saturated with an ultra-high-pure Ar gas for 30 min before the measurements, and kept bubbling Ar during the tests. For assessing the OER performance, the polarization curves were measured using the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) from 0.1 to 0.7 V versus SCE at 1 mV s−1, and the chronopotentiometric curve was recorded at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 in an O2-saturated 1 M KOH solution. However, for the HER performance, the polarization curves were measured from −1 V to −1.5 V versus SCE at 5 mV s−1 and the chronopotentiometric curve was recorded at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 in the Ar-saturated 1 M KOH solution. In addition, the polarization curve of the overall water-splitting was measured from 1.2 to 2.0 V at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1via a two-electrode configuration in 1 M KOH, and the chronopotentiometric curve was recorded at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2. Double layer capacitance was measured via cyclic voltammetry in the potential range from 0.96 V to 1.06 V vs. RHE. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at an overpotential of 237 mV from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz for OER and 132 mV from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz for HER. All the measured potentials of the electrocatalysts were referred to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) as follows: E (RHE) = E (SCE) + (0.059 × pH) + 0.241. All the LSV curves are corrected with an iR compensation.

The electrochemical tests were carried out using a standard three-electrode system with a CHI-760D electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, Shanghai, China). The platinum foil was used as the counter electrode and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode. In the performance tests of various catalysts for the HER and the OER, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 within a pH range of 0–14. The potential measured can be converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale using the following equation: ERHE = ESCE + E 0SCE + 0.059 pH, where, ESCE is the potential for experimental testing and E 0SCE = 0.242 V. In order to eliminate the effect of the ohmic resistance on the as-measured reaction currents, iR compensation was applied for all the electrochemical measurements. All tests were carried out at room temperature.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode electrochemical cell using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) with a 1 M KOH electrolyte. The as-synthesized electrode, Ag/AgCl, and a graphite rod were used as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. Polarization curves were obtained using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with a scan rate of 2 mV s−1 and were corrected for iR compensation. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were obtained by applying an AC voltage of 10 mV amplitude with frequency from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. Measurements of electrochemical double-layer capacitances were performed using cyclic voltammetry between 0.20 and 0.30 V. All potentials obtained were converted to the corresponding values based on the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the following equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.0591 × pH + 0.21 V. For the water and urea electrolysis tests, the V–Ni3N/NF was used as both the cathode and anode in a two-electrode electrolyzer.

Electrodes were printed from a conductive carbon-PLA filament (Protoplant) with water soluble polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) support structure (MatterHackers) on a MakerGear M3 FDM 3D-printer at 0.2 mm layer height and 100% infill at 50 mm s−1. Halfway through the printing of the electrodes, copper wire (Jameco Electronics, 24 AWG) was embedded along the length of the electrodes to improve base conductivity. Electrodes had a width of 1.7 cm and a length of 4.4 cm, giving a rectangular area of 7.5 cm2 when incorporated into the electrolyzer. The backsides of the electrodes were then masked with clear epoxy (JB Weld). Ni was deposited on these electrodes electrochemically via a Watt's nickel-plating solution comprised of 40.65 g of NiSO4·6H2O, 8.1 g of NiCl2·6H2O, and 5.4 g of H3BO3 added to 135 mL of deionized water. Electrodeposition was performed using a silver/silver chloride reference electrode (Hach) and a Ni foil counter electrode (99.5% purity, Alfa Aesar) at a constant current density of −2.5 mA cm−2 for three periods of two hours, resulting in deposition of a continuous Ni layer on the carbon-PLA substrate.

Electrocatalyst inks were prepared by mixing the active material with carbon black and Nafion. Specifically, 5 mg Ni-BDC/Ni(OH)2 hybrid nanosheets were dispersed in 1 mL H2O, and then 500 μL carbon black (2 mg ml−1) and 100 μL Nafion (1 wt%) were added into the solution and sonicated for 1 h. Glassy carbon electrodes with an area of 0.196 cm2 were polished to a mirror finish prior to use. Then, 10 μL of the electrocatalyst ink was drop-cast onto the electrode and the electrocatalyst layer was dried overnight. To permit comparison, Ni(OH)2, Ni-BDC and Ir/C inks were prepared using the same method and loaded on the glassy carbon electrode, except that no carbon black was added for Ir/C.

For comparison, commercial Pt/C and RuO2 on CP-based electrodes were also fabricated. The catalyst ink was produced by dissolving Pt/C (5.0 mg) or RuO2 (5.0 mg) in a solution of isopropyl alcohol (750 μL), DI water (200 μL) and Nafion (5%, 50 μL) under ultrasonication for 30 min. Then the ink was dropped on the surface of a pre-cleaned CP (1 cm × 1 cm) to obtain a catalyst content of 3.6 mg cm−2 followed by drying at 60 °C for 10 h.

All electrochemical measurements were carried out with a CHI 760E electrochemical station. The as-prepared Ni–Co–Se, Ni–Se, Co–Se and NF with 1 cm2 were employed as the working electrode. Platinum foil (99.99%, 1 cm2) and a SCE were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. All the measured potentials were calibrated to the value vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the equation ERHE = ESCE + 0.241 + 0.0591 pH. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots were tested in N2-saturated 1.0 M KOH for the OER with 95% IR compensation, and in the parallel electrolyte with 0.1 M N2H4·H2O for HzOR without IR compensation at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Tafel plots were derived from LSV curves by plotting the overpotential against the logarithm of current density. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the frequency range of 10−2 Hz–105 Hz with 5 mV amplitude. The double layer capacitance (Cdl) was tested by CV cycles in a non-faradaic potential window of −50 to 50 mV (vs. SCE) at various scan rates (10–50 mV s−1) to evaluate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the catalysts. For long-term stability of the catalysts, the chronopotentiometry measurements and multi-current steps were performed in 1.0 M KOH for the OER and 1.0 M KOH with 3.0 M N2H4·H2O for HzOR. The amount of O2 was measured by gas chromatography every 10 min and Clark electrode monitoring in the electrolyte.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies were carried out over a frequency range of 0.01–100 kHz over a bias potential range of 1.52 V to 1.67 V vs. RHE for the OER and −0.18 V to −0.33 V vs. RHE for the HER. All LSVs were iR corrected through EIS technology and via the equation: ECorrected = ERaw − IRs.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode setup with a Hg/HgO electrode as the reference electrode and a graphite rod (6 mm) as the counter electrode in 1 M KOH. Potentials were referenced to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE): ERHE = EHg/HgO + 0.098 + 0.0592 × pH (pH = 14). The overpotential (η) was calculated according to: η = ERHE − 1.23 V. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed in N2-saturated 1.0 M KOH at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 to obtain the polarization curves. Before formal electrochemical tests, the working electrodes were preconditioned by several cycles of voltammetric scans between 0 and 0.6 V vs. Hg/HgO. The chronoamperometric tests were performed under the same experimental setup with no iR compensation. The stability tests were performed by chronoamperometry at the overpotential corresponding to the current density of 20 mA cm−2 or 40 mA cm−2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was carried out at 0.6 V vs. Hg/HgO over a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) were measured according to literature methods.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode system using the electrochemical workstation (CHI1140C). Mercury/mercurous oxide (Hg/HgO) served as the reference electrode and a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode. The catalytic performances for the OER were investigated through linear sweep voltammetry polarization in O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH electrolyte. Before the measurements, cyclic voltammetry was carried out with a sweep rate of 50 mV s−1 for 50 cycles. All the measured potentials vs. the Hg/HgO electrode were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by the Nernst equation (ERHE = EHg/HgO + 0.0591pH + 0.098). The equilibrium potential (E0) for the OER is 1.23 V vs. RHE, so the overpotential is the potential value between ERHE and 1.23 V. To measure the ECSA of the electrodes, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was extracted from the generic cyclic voltammetry plots measured in a narrow potential window of 0.38 V to 0.48 V vs. RHE where no faradaic reactions occurred. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was measured at an overpotential of 300 mV from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 10 mV.

5 mg of catalysts was dispersed in the solution of 0.98 mL isopropyl alcohol mixed with 0.02 mL 5 wt% Nafion solution and then sonicated for 0.5 h. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 5 mm in diameter) was polished with 1.0, 0.5 and 0.05 μm alumina powder in sequence to obtain a mirror-like surface, and then washed with ultrapure water and ethanol by sonication. After drying in air, a definite volume of the ink was drop-cast on the surface of the GCE to form a catalyst film with a loading of about 0.306 mg cm−2.

All electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660D electrochemical working station (Chenhua Corp., China) at room temperature (25 °C). The catalysts were measured in 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution using a typical three-electrode configuration, in which CoyFezNi100−y−zOx/NF, CoyNi100−yOx/NF, FeyNi100−yOx/NF and NiO/NF (cut into 1 × 1 cm pieces with a loading of ∼4 mg cm−2) were used as the working electrode; platinum wire and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were acquired at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves were acquired at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 with 90% iR-compensation unless otherwise mentioned. The mass activity of the catalyst was calculated by the measured current density (mA cm−2) divided by the loading amount of catalyst (mg cm−2). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were measured at the open-circuit potential in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. Chronoamperometric curves were recorded at a constant current density without iR-compensation. The electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs) were determined from the capacitance measurements in the potential region of no faradic process at different scan rates of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 mV s−1. Commercial RuO2 and Pt/C-loaded electrodes were fabricated as benchmarks for comparison of the OER and HER performance of the catalyst, respectively. 20 mg of RuO2 or Pt/C was ultrasonically scattered in 0.2 mL of Nafion solution (0.5 wt%) and 0.8 mL of ethanol. The dispersion was transferred onto Ni foam by a controlled drop casting approach to obtain a loading of 4 mg cm−2. Overall water splitting tests were performed in a two-electrode configuration with Co6.25Fe18.75Ni75Ox as both anode and cathode by acquiring linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves with 90% iR-compensation at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and chronoamperometric curves at a constant current density without iR-compensation.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (AUTOLAB 204) using a three-electrode set-up consisting of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) loaded with one of the synthesized electrocatalyst as the working electrode, a glassy carbon rod as a counter electrode, and Hg/HgO (1 M KOH) as the reference electrode. First, 75 μg cm−2 of the electrocatalyst was loaded on the GCE over a working area of 0.196 cm2. The catalyst slurry contained 5 mg of the synthesized catalyst, 1000 μL of isopropyl alcohol, and 50 μL of Nafion solution. A linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique was used to evaluate the OER activity in 1 M KOH. The catalysts were linearly polarized from 1.2 to 1.8 V vs a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted from 100 000 to 0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 0.2 mA at 10 mA cm−2 for each electrode. Furthermore, durability of the catalysts for the OER was investigated by repeating the LSV over 500 cycles. All electrochemical results were calibrated and are reported vs RHE.

MANGAN Standard Electrocatalytic Testing Procedure: The eletrocatalytic properties of the synthesized materials were determined for the OER in an air-saturated alkaline (1 m KOH) solution at a constant temperature of 25 °C, controlled by a cryostat (Lauda Compact Thermostats RCS 20-D). We have applied a rotating electrode standardized protocol established in the framework of the MANGAN project, a German cluster research consortium funded by the German Ministry of Research and Education (BMBF) which, among other objectives, develops generic methods for comparison of electrocatalytic results across 26 research groups. The complete procedure is summarized in Scheme S2 (Supporting Information), while an example of the results obtained for a selected electrocatalyst is summarized in Figure S17 (Supporting Information). Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a compact and modular potentiostat/galvanostat Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT 204 device run on the NOVA software and equipped with an impedance module jacketed glass-free electrochemical 3-electrode half-cell reactor. A Hg/HgO electrode and Pt wire were used as a reference electrode (RE) and a counter electrode (CE), respectively. The working electrode (WE) was a rotating disk electrode (RDE) consisting of a glassy carbon disk electrode (4 mm diameter) in a polyether ether ketone (PEEK) sheath.

The electrochemical performances of the catalysts for OER and HER reactions were examined using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation equipped with a 3-electrode system. A graphite electrode (ø = 5 mm) was used as the counter electrode, with Hg/HgO electrode as a reference electrode. The as-obtained Mo2C/CoMoS4, CoMoS4, and Mo2C coated on glass carbon electrode (ø = 3 mm) were used as the working electrode. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted in 1 m sodium hydroxide solution with the scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The catalyst stability was tested by the chronoamperometry method under 10 mA cm−2. All the potentials reported in this work were transformed to values versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), calibrated by the following equation: E (vs RHE) = 0.095 + 0.059 pH + E (vs Hg/HgO). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded at the open-circuit voltage in 0.1–100 kHz with 5 mV of amplitude.

The electrochemical HER and OER performance were tested in a three-electrode system (Electrochemical workstation, Autolab-Metrohm), where Ag/AgCl used as a reference electrode and Pt wire was working as the counter electrode. For the better understanding the effect of the Pt and Graphite as counter electrode for HER analysis, the Linear-Swipe-Voltammetry (LSV) has been tested and found no significant change, shown in Figure S16. The HER and OER performance were measured in both 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1 M KOH electrolyte solution respectively with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. All measured potentials were converted with respect to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the following equation: ERHE=0.197+0.059xpH+E Ag/AgCl. The pH value of the electrolyte was measured before the electrochemical measurement. The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 6 mg mixture of g-C3N4 and Cu3P with the weight ratios (1 : 1.5; 1 : 1; 1:0.7; 1:0.5 and 1:0.2) were sonicated for 12 hours separately in a solution of 1 mL DI water and ethanol (1 : 1). Further, 10 μl of Nafion (0.5 wt. % in lower aliphatic alcohols and water, Sigma Aldrich) was added into the all individually prepared solutions. The as-prepared g-C3N4/Cu3P solutions were dropped cast (100 μl) on top of the five sets of 3D Graphene sheets (1.1×1.1 cm2).

The detail schematic for the preparation of g-C3N4/Cu3P on 3DG has been shown in Figure 2. The best catalytic performance was observed for 1 : 1 mixture (3DG-Mix) in 0.5 M H2SO4 and in case of 1 M KOH the optimized ratio was 1 : 1.15 for HER analysis. However, the optimized ratio was found to be 1:0.8 for OER analysis in 1 M KOH medium. A similar approach was followed for pure Cu3P and g-C3N4 samples (6 mg of each) was sonicated in 1 ml DI water and ethanol solution and mixed with 10 μl of Nafion. Further 7 μl of these solutions were dropped cast on glassy carbon electrode (0.07 cm2 area) separately. All the as-prepared electrodes were dried under IR lamp. The sample loading percentage for all the electrodes were ∼0.5 mg/cm2. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was also tested for the estimation of charge transfer resistance and series resistance in a three-electrode system over a frequency range 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz with an AC amplitude of 10 mV at a constant voltage 170 mV (vs RHE).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and long-term stability tests were conducted on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation using the asobtained electrocatalyst as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum plate (for OER) or a graphite rod (for HER) as the counter electrode. Prior to the OER measurement, the electrolyte solution was bubbled with high purity O2 for 30 min. Before the HER measurement, the 1 m KOH aqueous solution was degassed by bubbling N2. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was estimated according to the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) by performing CV tests at different scan rates (v in mV s−1) of 10–100 mV s−1 in the non-Faradaic potential region. Accordingly, the Cdl value was obtained according to the equation ic=νCdl.

The electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode configuration in 0.1 m KOH electrolyte. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3 mm was employed as working electrode, a Pt plate and an Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. For the preparation of catalysts ink, 2 mg catalysts were dispersed in a mixed solution of 0.2 mL water, 0.3 mL ethanol and 0.02 mL 5 wt% Nafion, and then sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous ink. Afterward, 3.6 μL of the catalyst ink was dropped onto the polished GCE. To evaluate the OER performance, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were obtained with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The Tafel slopes were acquired from the corresponding LSV curves. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were analyzed over the frequency range from 100 000 to 0.1 Hz. And the stability tests for the catalysts were performed with a chronopotentiometry method for 40 000 s at a current density of 10 mA cm−2.

The 3DP GC electrode was printed by an extrusion-based 3D printing method. The homemade 3D printer is modified from an industrial 3D robotic dispenser system (Fisnar F5200N benchtop gantry, Wayne, NJ) with the capability for programmable patterning in three dimensions. A high-precision pressure controller was used to provide controllable printing pressure to extrude ink filaments smoothly. In the experiments, the nozzle diameter, pressure, and nozzle's moving speed were 400 µm, 180–360 kPa, and 5–30 mm s−1, respectively. The target patterns were printed onto glass wafers in air at room temperature. It takes approximately 160 s to print 18 layers of 3DP GC electrodes with a size of 1 × 2 cm−2. After printing, the printed architectures were frozen in a −80 °C refrigerator for 0.5 h and lyophilized for 12 h. Then, the samples were thermally reduced at 900 °C in N2 for 120 min with a heating/cooling rate of 5 °C min−1 to obtain 3DP GC electrodes. Under the same conditions as the preparation of 3DP GC, T-3DP GC with a nonporous microscopic surface was fabricated by controlling the GO reduction time, and bulk GC with a porous microscopic surface was prepared by adjusting the 3D printing program.

All measurements of electrochemical performance were investigated on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) in a three-electrode system at room temperature, in which the obtained electrodes were employed as the working electrode, graphite rods (HERs), Pt plates (OERs) acted as counter electrodes and saturated calomel electrodes served as reference electrodes. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curve was obtained with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. All the obtained data were corrected by 100% iR compensation, and the potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), ERHE=ESCE+0.059pH+0.242. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was obtained in a KOH electrolyte with a frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz. The ECSA was collected by CV at scan rates ranging from 1 to 5 mV s−1. The overall water splitting performance was studied in a two-electrode system. The 3DP GC/Ni-NiO electrode worked as the cathode, and the 3DP GC/NiS2 acted as the anode. The stability of the electrode was tested under 10 mA cm−2 at room temperature.

Electrochemical measurement was performed on a rotating ring-disk electrode controlled by a CHI bipotentiostat. Pt wire and Hg/HgO, KOH (0.1 M) were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode respectively. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 2 mg of catalysts was dispersed into 2 mL of water and isopropanol solution (3 : 1, v : v) containing 10 μL of Nafion (5%, Sigma-Aldrich). This mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous ink, then 12 μL of the catalyst ink was cast onto the glassy carbon disk at a loading density of ~0.1 mg/cm2. Linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) was recorded at a scanning rate of 10 mV/s under continuous rotation (1600 rpm). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was recorded at a scanning rate of 30 or 250 mV/s. Tafel plot was obtained by recording the steady-state current at applied potentials descending in steps of 10 mV when the electrode was rotated at 1600 rpm. In this study, potential (E) was converted to reversible hydrogen electrode and corrected with iR compensation (R is the ohmic resistance of electrolyte and electric circuit components measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was measured in a range of frequency from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz with the amplitude of 5 mV. Overpotential was computed using η = E(reversible hydrogen electrode) − 1.23 V.

The electrochemical performance was tested on a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Shanghai CH Instrument) fabricating with a composite material working electrode, a graphite counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. EIS was conducted at open circuit potential, with AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV and frequency from 105 to 0.01 Hz. LSV curves were obtained at the second sweep in the range of 1.2 to 2.0 V (vs RHE) at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 in 1.0 m of Na2SO4 electrolyte, and corrected by 91% iR compensation for stable current concern. The stability of the electrocatalyst was explored by 3000 CV scans from 1.0 to 1.6 V (vs RHE) at a scanning rate of 200 mV s−1. The Eonset for OER was defined as the critical potential while the current density is 0.5 mA cm−2. Converted to an RHE accorded to test correction, the overpotential (η) was calculated according to the formula η (V) = ERHE−1.23.

In order to optimize the electrodeposition time of catalysts toward the HER and OER, FeCoNiP1S0 catalysts with deposition times of 30, 60, 75, 90, 105, 120, and 150 s were prepared and evaluated. The optimized deposition time of FeCoNiP1S0 was determined to be 120 s for the HER and 105 s for the OER, respectively. As a result, all of the FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were prepared at 120 s for the HER and 105 s for the OER. In order to investigate the roles played by Fe, Co, and Ni for FeCoNiPxSy catalysts toward the HER and OER, MP0S1 (M: Fe, Co, Ni, FeCo, FeNi, and CoNi) samples with a deposition duration of 120 s for the HER and MP0.5S0.5 with a deposition duration of 105 s for the OER were prepared in their deposition solutions, respectively, in which the total content of metal salts was maintained at 0.173 M while the compositions of the metals were systematically varied.

The coral-like Ni3S2 was synthesized by electrochemical anodization and cathodic deposition method in a two-electrode cell with two pieces of NF (2 cm × 3 cm) as the anodic and cathodic electrodes. NF was successively ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 20 min. A complex aqueous solution containing 1.0 M NH4F and 2.0 M thiourea was used as the electrolyte. The electrochemical anodization and cathodic deposition process were carried out at a constant potential of 4 V on a dc voltage constant-current power supply for various times of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h. The samples prepared for different times of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h are denoted as Ni3S2/NF-1, Ni3S2/NF-2, Ni3S2/NF-3, Ni3S2/NF-4, and Ni3S2/NF-5, respectively. The masses of loaded Ni3S2 are about 2.0, 2.3, 2.5, 2.8, and 3.3 mg cm–2 for Ni3S2/NF-1, Ni3S2/NF-2, Ni3S2/NF-3, Ni3S2/NF-4, and Ni3S2/NF-5, respectively.

The typical synthetic procedure for the catalysts is illustrated in Scheme 1. First, a NiFe0.10Cr0.10 alloy film was deposited on Cu NA by cathodic electrolysis of an aqueous solution containing NiCl2, Fe(NO3)3, and Cr(NO3)3 under an applied potential of −0.9 V vs RHE for 300 s. With the molar ratio of Ni2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ adjusted to 1:0.10:0.10, an electrodeposited film denoted as NiFe0.10Cr0.10 was obtained. Then, the obtained electrode was immersed in 1 M KOH electrolyte for activation. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans between 1.0 and 1.8 V versus RHE were conducted to activate the surface of the NiFe0.10Cr0.10 film to an OER-active hydroxide phase, which is denoted as CS-NiFe0.10Cr0.10 (Figure S1). For comparison, CS-NiFe0.10 samples without Cr incorporation were also prepared.

Ni(OH)2, Fe-doped Ni(OH)2, and Al-doped Ni(OH)2 were deposited onto FNC or fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrodes through electrodeposition. Ni, NiFe, and NiAl catalysts were prepared by starting from 100 mL of a 0.1 M solution of Ni(NO3)2 or by adding 0.2 mmol of either FeCl3·6H2O or AlCl3·6H2O to the Ni(NO3)2 solution, respectively. Prior to electrodeposition, the pH of the solutions was adjusted to 6.6 and the system was purged with nitrogen gas for 1 h. Electrodeposition was performed in a three-electrode cell configuration at −16 mA/cm2 for 10 s, by using a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) and a Pt mesh as the counter electrode (CE).

MnOx catalyst films were prepared by controlled-potential electrolysis of solutions containing 0.5 mM Mn2+ and 50 mM methylphosphonate (MePi) at pH 8.0. These solutions remained clear over the course of all depositions. A FTO-coated glass slide was masked to 1 cm2 and immersed in the quiescent solution. A constant potential of 0.537 V was applied to the electrode with automatic iR compensation until the total charge passed reached the desired loading of catalyst. For example, a 6 mC/cm2 loading required ∼700 s of deposition time. Following deposition, films were immersed in reagent water to remove any trace Mn2+ and MePi remaining from solution. The films were prevented from drying by quickly transferring them to a Mn2+-free solution for subsequent experiments.

The Co–Mo–P–-O was electrodeposited on Ni foam substrates using a three-electrode electrochemical system. The electrolyte was an aqueous solution containing 0.15 M CoSO4, 0.05 M Na2MoO4, 0.15 M Na3C6H5O7, and 0.5 M NaH2PO2. The Ni foam, Pt foil, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. Prior to electrodeposition, the Ni foam of 2 cm × 2 cm was first degreased by successive 15-min ultrasonication in acetone and absolute ethanol. It then was etched in 1.0 M HCl for 15 min to remove the surface oxides. Finally, the Ni foam was rinsed by abundant distilled water and dried by N2. The electrochemical deposition was performed under a constant potential of −2 V (vs SCE) at room temperature for 5 min. The total loading amount of the as-electrodeposited Co–Mo–P–O was measured to be 3.06 mg cm–2 through weighting the weight change before and after electrodeposition. A contrast sample, called the as-electrodeposited Co-sample, was prepared using the same procedure, except the electrolyte only contains 0.15 M CoSO4 and 0.15 M Na3C6H5O7.

The FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were prepared by electrodeposition on a planar Ti foil at −1 A cm–2 without stirring. For the deposition of FeCoNiPxSy, NiSO4, FeSO4, and CoSO4 were used as the metal salts and NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2 were used as the salts for introducing P and S dopants. Moreover, H3BO3 and NH4Cl were added to the deposition solution as additional agents to improve the quality of the deposits. The electrolyte bath contained 0.09 M NiSO4, 0.03 M FeSO4, 0.053 M CoSO4, 0.4 M H3BO3, 0.28 M NH4Cl, and certain amounts of NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2. The Fe:Co:Ni metal ratio was maintained at 17:31:52 because this ratio has been reported to afford catalysts with the best electrochemical performance.  When the P and S compositions of FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were tuned, the total amounts of NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2 were maintained at 1 M while those of NaH2PO2 (x M) and H2NCSNH2 (y M) were systematically varied and the resulting samples are denoted FeCoNiPxSy. These samples were FeCoNiP1S0, FeCoNiP0.8S0.2, FeCoNiP0.5S0.5, FeCoNiP0.2S0.8, and FeCoNiP0S1, which were prepared with a two-electrode electrodeposition.

A NF was cleaned by sonicating in 1 M HCl solution for 30 min, followed by ethanol for 15 min, cleaned subsequently with DI water, and then left to dry in air. The series of Ni1–xFex nanosheets (x: 0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1) were prepared by electrodeposition using a three-electrode cell in which NF is used as the working electrode. Platinum wire and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For electrodeposition, 6 mM Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 6 mM Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were used, and the ratio between the precursors were varied to obtain a series of Ni1–xFex nanosheets. Deposition was carried out by applying −1.0 V for 300 s. This process led to the incorporation of bulk Fe (FeB). Once electrodeposition is completed, the material is rinsed with DI water and left to dry. After that, FeB was cycled in 1 M Fe(NO3)3 containing 1 M KOH to incorporate surface Fe (FeS).

The amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are synthesized by a simple, facile, and green electrochemistry technique (Supporting Information, S1).  Two parallel graphite electrodes are used as the cathode and anode electrodes immersed in a quartz chamber, and the distance between two electrodes is about 5 cm. A target of 99.99% pure nickel with an area of 25 × 25 mm2 is fixed at the center of a quartz chamber and immersed in highly pure deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm–1) without any chemical additives. The synthesis is carried out under a constant potential voltage of 90 V for 75 min, and the final products are deposited on the surface of the graphite sheet (electrode). The detailed formation of the amorphous Ni(OH)2 is provided in the Supporting Information (S1).

NiCo-CH NW/NF and α-Ni(OH)2 NS/NF were transformed into NiCoP NW/NF and Ni2P NS/NF, respectively, through a low-temperature phosphorization procedure in a tube furnace. In detail, a porcelain boat filled with 500 mg of NaH2PO2 was placed at the upstream position of the tube furnace, while the sample was placed at the downstream position (the center of the heat source). Subsequently, the sample was heated at 300 °C with a slow heating rate of 2 °C min–1 and maintained for 2 h with continuous argon gas flow, and then cooled naturally to ambient temperature.

Ir thin films were deposited in a traveling-wave-type ALD reactor (NCD Technology, Lucida D100, Korea) using the TICP precursor and O2 (99.99%). TICP was synthesized by TANAKA Precious Metals (Japan) by a previously reported process.  The range of deposition temperature was 200–325 °C. A standard cycle of the Ir ALD process consisted of precursor pulsing for 7 s, reactant pulsing for 5 s, and purging for 10 s. This process was established according to the self-limiting growth criteria discussed below. The precursor was heated at 55 °C to obtain a suitable vapor pressure during film deposition. N2 (100 sccm) was used as the carrier and purge gas for the metal precursor and was also used between two consecutive pulses during deposition. Deposition was typically conducted on H-terminated Si substrates. Furthermore, the growth behavior on the following surfaces was also investigated: SiO2 (100 nm), sputtered TiN (thickness: 20 nm on Si), a Ru film grown by ALD using tricarbonyl(trimethylenemethane)ruthenium [Ru(TMM) (CO)3] as a precursor and O2 as a reactant (thickness: 10 nm on Si),  and Al2O3 obtained by ALD using trimethylaluminum [Al(CH3)3] as the precursor and H2O as the reactant (thickness: 10 nm/Si).  The Ru film also acted as the seed layer for Ir ALD in some experiments.

Regarding SrMnO2.6, a previous report on the synthesis of this compound included at least four steps, which involved switching between different gases, various temperatures, and gas mixtures for each step.  However, we have found a one-step synthesis method for this material as well. We synthesized SrMnO2.6 through heat treatment at 1200 °C for 24 h in argon. The extensive flushing of the tube furnace prior to heating, which was done for the previous material, was not done here. Also, a sacrificial pellet of MnO2 with the same size as that of the sample (∼10 mm diameter and ∼2 mm thickness) was placed near the sample, about 20 mm apart, in the direction of an argon flow. In other words, the MnO2 pellet was closer to the argon source. This arrangement allowed a small amount of oxygen, which was released from MnO2, to be carried toward the sample pellet. This one-step process provides an easy route to the synthesis of SrMnO2.6, instead of the four-step process reported before.

To synthesize the CoFeS (Se or P) frame/cagelike structure, the CoFe PBA precursor was annealed under different S, Se, and P vapors. Briefly, 50 mg of CoFe PBA and 1 g of sulfur (500 mg of selenium or 1 g of sodium hypophosphite) powder were put separately into two porcelain boats at the upstream side of the tube furnace under Ar gas. The CoFeS (Se or P) frame/cagelike structures were acquired by the annealing process in two steps. First, the tube furnace was heated at 150 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min–1, and then the temperature was increased to 350 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C min–1 and was kept for 3.30 h.

Typically, 0.3 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 12 mmol CH4N2S, and 23 mg NGF were mixed into 70 mL distilled water and stirred for 30 min, then the mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain MoS2/NGF precipitate. As for the synthesis of NiS2/NGF, Ni(OH)2/NGF was firstly obtained by using the same hydrothermal treatment to MoO2-Ni(OH)2/NGF but without the presence of Na2MoO4·2H2O. Then, NiS2/NGF was synthesized by sulfuration of Ni(OH)2/NGF, which process was similar to the sulfuration of the above MoO2-Ni(OH)2/NGF.

20 mg CoCPNFs and 1 g NaH2PO2·H2O were placed at both ends of the ceramic boat. Then, the porcelain boat is placed in a tubular furnace and the porcelain boat containing NaH2PO2·H2O is located at upstream. Whereafter, the tube furnace was heated to 350 °C at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 and maintained for 2 h under argon protection. The obtained black powder is denoted as CoP@NCNFs. Furthermore, in order to investigate the effect of different phosphorylation amounts on the catalytic activity, CoP@NCNFs-0.5 and CoP@NCNFs-1.5 were also prepared with the similar steps except the addition amount of sodium hypophosphite during phosphorylation (Table S1).

The as-prepared NiCo-LDH/Ni foam and 100 mg selenium powder were loaded into a ceramic boat and the selenium powder was placed at the upstream of the ceramic boat. Subsequently, the samples were placed at the center of the tube furnace. The sample was then heated at 450 °C for 2 h under the protection of Ar gas with a heating rate of 5 °C/min. As a control, we also prepared a sample with 200 mg of Se powder.

The iron oxide deposited on commercial Ni foam was carried out by the ALD technique (Ensure Scientific). During the deposition process, ferrocene and O3 were used as the iron and oxygen source, respectively. The reactor and the ferrocene source were maintained at 250 and 80 °C, respectively. For each ALD cycle, the pulse time and purging time for ferrocene and O3 were 1 s/15 s and 0.5 s/8 s, respectively. After that, the nickel foam with iron oxide and NaH2PO2·H2O were placed separately in a porcelain boat. Then, the sample was heated to 300 °C under the flowing nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h and then cooled to ambient temperature.

Nickel foams with dimensions of 1 × 0.12 × 4 cm3 were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and water for 10 min, sequentially. After drying, the foams were placed into a quartz tube furnace for CVD growth of graphene nanosheets. The samples were heated to 1080 °C with a heating rate of 15 °C.min−1 under 45.5 sccm Ar/H2 gas flow (9:1 vol/vol). After annealing for 15 min, 20 sccm CH4 gas was blown for 15 min. Afterward, the furnace was turned off and the feeding of methane gas was stopped. The specimens were taken outside of the heating zone of the furnace and cooled under the Ar/H2 gas flow.

Synthesis of Co3S4/MnS2: 160 mg Co(NO3)2∙6H2O and 160 mg MnN2O6∙4H2O were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol and stirred for another 20 min. After the solution was completely mixed, 320 mg TAA was added to the mixture, stirred for 15 min, transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, the treated NF was added to the autoclave, and then reacted at 160 °C for 3 h.

Prior to synthesis, Ni foam (2 cm × 4 cm × 1 cm) is sonicated in 3 M HCl solution for 15 min to remove the oxide layer attached to the surface, then washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for three times.
