The CoMoO4/NF precursor is prepared by a facile hydrothermal process. Firstly, 50 mL of distilled water is used to dissolve 1.45 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.88 g (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O. Meanwhile, 1.2 g CO(NH2)2 is also added as an surfactant. The total solution system is magnetic stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, transfer the above mixed solution to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and soak the treated NF into it. Then the autoclave is sealed at 120 °C for 10 h. After that, the NF coated with purple product, namely CoMoO4/NF precursor is collected. It is cleaned with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water alternatively and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

First, dissolve 0.3 g Fe(NO3)3 and 0.05 g S in the mixture of 15 ml distilled water, 5 ml ethylene glycol and 30 ml toluene and stir the mixed solution at the rate of 400 r min−1 for 2 h at room temperature. The prepared solution was then transferred to the 100 ml PTFE reactor. Next, successively put 5 mm * 5 mm Ni foam into anhydrous ethanol, acetone, 2 mol L−1 dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water for 15 min to obtain clean Ni foam. To ensure that the Co, S-NiFe2O4 grows evenly on the front and back of the Ni foam, the treated Ni foam needs to be welded to the nickel strip, and then the nickel strip is bent so that it acts as the upholder to make the nickel foam stand upright in the 100 ml PTFE reactor liner containing the reaction solution. After assembling the reaction kettle, put it into the muffle furnace, set the temperature to 150 ℃ with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1, and heat it for 5 h; after cooling to room temperature, take out the reaction kettle. Co, S-NiFe2O4 was grown on nickel foam, and eventually rinse Ni foam repeatedly with ethanol and dry it.

NiMoO4 @NF precursor was in-situ grown on the surface of NF via a facile hydrothermal approach. Nickel foam (NF) (1 cm × 2 cm) was first immersed and then sonicated in 0.1 M H2SO4, acetone solution, and ethanol solution for 20 min to remove the oxide layer on the surface, then the cleaned NF were dried in a vacuum oven. Then, the same mole ratio of Ni(NO3)20.6 H2O and Na2MoO40.2 H2O were dissolved in 30 ml deionized water under stirring until completely dissolved. Afterward, the dried NF were subsequently immersed into the above solution, and then were transferred into 50 ml of Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling down, the as-obtained sample were taken out and washed with ethanol and DI water for several times, and the NiMoO4 @NF precursor were obtained after drying.

Fe foam (FF) was washed by 1 mol/L of HCl, acetone and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight. Then 8 pieces of cleaned FFs (1 ×1.5 cm2; Fig. S1), 5 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O and 25 mmol of urea were put into 50 mL deionized (DI) water with 30 min ultrasonication to promote salt dispersion. The mixture was then transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 90 °C for 12 h to obtain CoFe-LDH/FF, which was subsequently washed by DI water, ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

The synthesis procedure for Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN product is illustrated in Scheme 1. The hydrothermal synthesis process of Mo doped Ni, Fe precursor was performed at 120 °C for 12 h, followed by an annealing treatment at 380 °C for 3 h in NH3 atmosphere. The as-obtained product is denoted as Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN. For comparison, the same synthesis procedure is adopted for synthesis of Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN without the addition of Mo.

In a typical synthesis, 0.3982 g of Ni(acac)2, 0.0795 g of FeCl2, 0.05 g of MoNa2O4, and 0.5608 g of C6H12N4 were dissolved in 35 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the precursor was washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried at 80 °C overnight. The as-obtained powder was calcined at 380 °C for 3 h under NH3 atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The other catalysts can be also obtained with different molar ratios of Mo/Ni/Fe.

Ni3S4 hollow nanoprisms were synthesized via a solvothermal method. In total, the above Ni precursor nanoprisms (96 ​mg) was redispersed into ethanol (48 ​ml) with the addition of thioacetamide (TAA, 135 ​mg), which was averagely transferred into three sealed Teflon-lined autoclaves (20 ​mL) at 120 ​°C for 6 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black precipitated product was harvested via centrifugation at 6000 ​rpm and washed 3 times with ethanol.

La2LiIrO6 and La2LiRuO6 were synthesized by solid-state reaction of the precursors La2O3 (Alfa Aesar 99.99%) previously dried at 1,000 ∘C overnight, Li2CO3 (Sigma-Aldrich > 99%), IrO2 (Alfa Aesar 99%) and RuO2 (Alfa Aesar 99.9%), respectively, and fired at 900 ∘C under O2 for 24 h with intermediate grinding for La2LiIrO6 and 980 ∘C for 24 h with intermediate grinding for La2LiRuO6. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by means of a BRUKER D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation. The crystallographic parameters were determined by means of the Le Bail method using the FullProf suite.

Na2CoP2O7 and NaCoPO4 powders were synthesized using the conventional solid-state method. Stoichiometric amounts of Na2CO3 (ACS reagent, ⩾99%, Aldrich), CoC2O4 (⩾99%, Kojundo) and (NH4)2HPO4 (ACS reagent 98%, Aldrich) were wet-ball milled in acetone media and heated at 300 °C under Ar media for 6 h. After grinding, the pelletized samples were calcined at 600 °C and 750 °C under Ar media for 6 h for Na2CoP2O7 and NaCoPO4, respectively. Li2CoP2O7 and LiCoPO4 powders were synthesized using the stoichiometric amounts of Li2CO3 (ACS reagent, ⩾99%, Aldrich), CoC2O4 (⩾99%, Kojundo) and (NH4)2HPO4 (ACS reagent 98%, Aldrich). The precursors were mixed by ball milling, heated at 300 °C under Ar media for 6 h, pelletized and calcined at 600 °C for Li2CoP2O7 and 800 °C for LiCoPO4 under Ar media for 6 h. Amorphous Co-Pi was synthesized according to the previous report.

Si-incorporated perovskite samples were prepared by a ball-milling-assisted solid-state reaction approach. Freshly dried chemicals of SrCO3, Co3O4 and SiO2 (Sigma-Aldrich) were weighted according to the stoichiometric ratio of SrCo1–ySiyO3–δ (y = 0.00, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.10) with different intentional Si-doping levels. The precursory powders were then mixed in an acetone medium for 1 h using a high-energy ball mill (Planetary Mono Mill, Pulverisette 6, Fritisch) at a rotation of 400 rpm. The as-obtained mixtures were dried, pressed into pellets and subjected to calcination in air under ambient pressure at 1000–1200 °C for 24 h with intermediate grindings. The actual composition of each sample after calcination was analysed by XRD (Supplementary Tables 1 and 2).

All composite oxides were synthesized with solid state method. Powders of SrCO3, IrO2, TiO2, and Co3O4 from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation were used as raw materials. Briefly, stoichiometry amount of raw materials were weighted and then mixed in mortar. The mixed precursors were finally calcined with box furnaces in ambient air. A sintering condition of 850 °C for 12 h, 1100 °C for 12° h, and 1200 °C for 12 h is applied for m-SrIrO3, SrCo0.9Ir0.1O3−δ, and SrCo0.9Ti0.1O3−δ respectively.

IOMO was prepared by means of a facile electrospinning method in the presence of ammonium molybdate (para) tetrahydrate, and polyvinylpyrrolidone (1.2 g, PVP, average M.W. 130,000), followed by high-temperature pyrolysis. Specifically, 1 mmol ammonium molybdate (para) tetrahydrate and 1.2 g of PVP were added to a mixed solvent of dimethylformamide (5 mL DMF) and alcohol (5 mL EtOH) with stirring at 80-degree for 12 h. The mixed solution was added into a 10 mL plastic syringe with a 25-gauge stainless-steel needle. The applied direct current voltage and distance between the needle and the aluminum-foil-wrapped collector were fixed at 15 kV and 12 cm, respectively. The flow rate of the mixed solution was maintained at 10 µL min−1. The as-prepared materials were mixed with IrO2 (1 mmol) and then were annealed at 500 °C for 3 h in the air at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Finally, IOMO was collected.

In a typical process, 20 mg of cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate was dissolved in 5 ml of dimethyl formamide (DMF) and sonicated for 3 minutes. The carbon foam sample (20 mg) which can be either A800 or P900 or CNTs/P900, was submerged into the mixture together with 65 μL of thiophene. The mixture was then sonicated for 20 minutes, followed by a drying at 125 °C under nitrogen flow. Finally, the obtained sample was annealed at 400 °C for 2 h in nitrogen atmosphere to facilitate the formation of CoOx. Depending on the initial carbon foam (A800, P900 and CNTs/P900), the final products were denoted as CoOx@A800, CoOx@P900 and CoOx@CNTs/P900, respectively. It is important to note that, a similar procedure (but without annealing step) was used to decorate cobalt on P900 which was later used to synthesis CNTs/P900.

The w-Ni(OH)2 was prepared through the exfoliation of the w-Ni(OH)2-e sample. Typically, 0.1 g of w-Ni(OH)2-e was added into 40 mL of ethanol and then the mixture was treated by ultrasound for 24 h. After that, the product was collected by centrifuging at a speed of 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min. The powder was washed by water and ethanol four times and then dried at 80 °C overnight. The obtained sample was named as w-Ni(OH)2.

NiyM(OH)x HNAs were fabricated using the Cu2O nanowire arrays as sacrificial templates. Briefly, known amounts of NiCl2·6 H2O and MCl2·nH2O (M = Fe or Zn) were dissolved in a mixture containing 17.5 mL Mill-Q water and 17.5 mL ethanol. A 2:1 molar ratio of NiCl2·6H2O and MCl2·nH2O was used to prepare the NiyM(OH)x HNAs. The total moles of NiCl2·6H2O and MCl2·nH2O used was 8 × 10−5. A Cu foam decorated with Cu2O nanowire arrays was immersed in this suspension, which was then stirred at room temperature. Na2S2O3 (1 M, 10 mL) was added dropwise to this mixture under magnetic stirring for 1 h. The substrate was then removed and washed repeatedly in ethanol and Mill-Q water before being dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for 4 h.

The (FexNi1−x)4N NPs were obtained by calcination. First of all, the as-prepared Ni-doped iron oxide precursor was ground into powder, 0.4 g of the precursor was put into a porcelain boat and placed in the center of the quartz tube in a tubular furnace. Another porcelain boat with 1.5 mL of ethylenediamine was placed 3 cm in front of the boat containing the precursor. Next, under the protection of N2, the tube furnace was heated to 560–660 °C at a rate of 20 °C min−1 and then held at the final temperature for 30 min, then it was naturally cooled to room temperature (RT). Finally, black powder products were collected after drying, washing and grinding.

ZIF-67/NF was prepared in accordance with the literature expected for the use of a nickel foam matrix. Typically, a piece of Ni foam (NF, 2 × 1 cm2) was first cleaned ultrasonically, in a 1 M HCl solution, ultrapure water and acetone for 30 min, respectively. Next, 50 mL of a dimethylimidazole aqueous solution, containing 1.642 g dimethylimidazole, was poured into a 50 mL Co(NO3)2 aqueous solution, containing 0.484 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O, to a uniform purple solution. After 2 min of ultrasonication, the pretreated NF was incubated in the above solution for 4 h. Then, the NF was taken out, and washed with adequate water and dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight to obtain the final product denoted as ZIF-67/NF.

A methanolic solution of Cu(NO3)2·6H2O was added to a suspension of ligand (2,2′-bipyridine N,N′-dioxide). The overall ratio of Cu : ligand was 1 : 3. A clear solution was obtained. This solution was filtered off and stood at room temperature. After a few days, some block crystals were obtained.

The MWCNTs were treated with an acid mixture under sonication for their functionalization. Specifically, 200 mg of MWCNTs were mixed in a 500 mL round bottle flask with 150 mL of HNO3 and 50 mL of H2SO4 (3 : 1 (v/v)) at 75 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the obtained black powder was separated by suction filtration and washed with deionized water until its pH value became approximately 7 before drying at 70 °C.

Co-precursors were synthesized according to the reported method with minor modifications. Typically, 0.512 g cobalt acetate tetrahydrate and 1.0 g polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = ∼58 K) were dissolved in 80 ml ethanol in a round-bottom flask under sonication to generate a pink solution. The obtained solution was heated to 85 °C and refluxed under continuous stirring for 4 hours. The precipitated product was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol ten times and dried at 60 °C.

40 mg of Co-precursor NCs was dispersed in 4 ml ethanol to obtain solution A. 40 mg of K3[Fe(CN)6] was first dispersed in 20 ml deionized water and then mixed with 20 ml ethanol to get solution B. After pouring solution B rapidly into solution A under continuous stirring at room temperature for 10 minutes, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol three times, and finally dried at 60 °C.

100 mg of K3[Co(CN)6] was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water within a centrifuge tube, and then 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 dilute HCl was added with ultrasound for 10 min. The above CH/CF was put into the solution under ambient conditions for 4 h, then taken out, rinsed and dried to obtain a Cu3[Co(CN)6]2 particle coated Cu(OH)2 sub-microwire array (denoted as CH@PBA/CF). Besides, the reaction conditions were optimized by adjusting the amount of dilute HCl solution (0.5,1, and 1.5 mL) and the reaction time (2, 4, and 6 h), respectively. For comparison, Cu3[Co(CN)6]2 particles on bare CF, denoted as PBA/CF, were prepared under the same conditions as those of CH@PBA/CF. The mass loadings of CH@PBA/CF and PBA/CF are 6.3 ± 0.4 and 3.9 ± 0.2 mg cm−2, respectively.

In a typical preparation, 0.404 g FeN3O9·9H2O was dissolved into 80 mL of deionization water and 20 mL of ethanol under stirring. The mixed aqueous solution was transferred into a 250 mL three-necked flask and heated at 60 °C in an oil bath for 2 hours. When the temperature of the oil bath reaches 60 °C, the prepared Ni/Ni foam was added to the three-necked flask over without stirring. After cooling down to room temperature, the NiFe(OH)X nanoparticles/Ni foam was washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times and then freeze-dried for 12 hours.

The Ni1−xLixO mixed oxides were prepared as described elsewhere through solid state reactions of oxidation–decomposition. Appropriate amounts of the precursors: Ni (99.99%, Aldrich) and Li2CO3 (99.9%, Aldrich) pellets, were mixed and mechanically activated by ball milling before the thermal treatment, which was performed in air at 850 °C for 12 h. Cylinders and disks were prepared by compressing (∼200 MPa) the powders in a mold (diameter 1 cm) and then sintering the compacted material for 4 h at 800 °C in air.

Polystyrene (PS) microspheres with an average diameter of 820 nm were synthesized via an emulsifier-free emulsion polymerization process. First, 1 L deionized water was deoxygenated in N2 at 65 °C for 12 h in a reactor equipped with a bladed pitched paddle impeller. Subsequently, 160 g styrene (99 wt%, Sigma-Aldrich), 0.005 g sodium 4-styrenesulfonate (90 wt%, Alfa Aesar), and 0.2 g potassium bicarbonate (99 wt%, J. T. Baker) were mixed to form a homogeneous solution. Afterward, 0.4 g potassium persulfate (99 wt%, Sigma-Aldrich) was added as the initiator and the solution was stirred at 370 rpm for 16 h at 60 °C. Lastly, the mixture was dried at 65 °C to evaporate residual solvent, and the remaining PS microspheres were re-suspended in anhydrous ethanol at 25 °C and sonicated for 24 h to form a homogeneous PS colloidal suspension of 0.5 wt% loading.

To prepare NiFe2O4-NFs, 0.08 M of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) was mixed in a bottle containing 2.2 ml of dimethylformamide (DMF). The solution was initially sonicated for 10 min and kept on a magnetic stirrer for 1 h to obtain a clear solution. Further, 0.05 M of nickel(ii) nitrate and 0.05 M of iron(ii) nitrate were mixed into it and the solution mixture was stirred for another 6 h. The resultant solution was considered a precursor solution for an electrospinning process, where the conditions were optimized as follows, a 2 mL syringe was used for the entire ES process. The flow rate of the precursor solution was set to 0.9 ml min−1, the applied voltage was 18–20 kV and the distance between the syringe needle and the collector was 12 cm. Under these optimized conditions, the fiber was obtained. Further, the obtained fiber was subjected to a calcination temperature of 350 °C at a scan rate of 5° min−1 in a box furnace. Finally, a spinal NiFe2O4 structure was identified in the carbonized fiber matrix. The resultant fiber was collected and denoted as NiFe2O4-NFs. The detailed procedure to make NiFe2O4-NFs is portrayed in Fig. 1a.

ZIF-67 and ruthenium acetylacetonate (3 : 1 weight ratio, respectively) were taken and ground well in a mortar-pestle. The obtained powder was added into a closed quartz tube and subjected to pyrolysis at 900 °C for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere in a Lenton tube furnace. The as-obtained black product was named as RuCo@NC. Palladium acetylacetonate and platinum acetylacetonate were used in place of ruthenium acetylacetonate to obtain PdCo@NC and PtCo@NC, respectively.

(i) The polycrystalline sample of Na4Co3P4O15 (SS) was synthesized using a conventional solid-state reaction. A starting mixture of NaPO3 and Co3O4 (4 : 1 molar ratio) was milled for 0.5 h in an agate mortar. The powder was transferred to a ceramic crucible and pre-heated at 400 °C for 2 h. Then, the powder was re-ground and finally calcined at the optimized temperature (700 °C) for 50 h.

Typically, 3 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O (99 %) and 4.5 mmol of trisodium citrate dihydrate (99 %) were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water under stirring. After that, 100 mL of deionized water containing 2 mmol of K3[Co(CN)6] (99 %) was added and stirred vigorously for 20 min. Then, aging was conducted for 48 h, and the as-obtained powdery product was centrifuged, rinsed several times with deionized water and ethanol (99.5 %), and finally dried at 40 °C under vacuum.

Ni foam was firstly degreased in a mixture of organic solvents (equivolumetric mixture of ethanol, acetone, and deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ, Millipore)) with an ultrasonicator for 0.5 h. The NF (geometric area, 1 × 2 cm2) substrate was further sonicated in 5 m HCl for 15 min to remove the NiOx impurity and sequentially rinsed with DI water and ethanol. The precursors for NFO were prepared by dissolving Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (99% Alfa-Aesar), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (99% Alfa-Aesar), and 10 mM urea in DI water at Ni to Fe molar ratio of 1:2 (total metal concentration: 25 mm) with a vigorous stirring over 5 h. The Ir precursor (25 mm Ir) was prepared with H2IrCl6·6H2O (40 wt.% of Ir, Alfa-Aesar) dissolved in ethanol/isopropanol solvent (1:1, v/v) with added 0.3 m HCl. The Ir precursor was added to the NFO precursor at 1–10 mol.% for Ir in Ir-NFO precursor. In the straightforward sol-gel method, the pretreated NF was dipped into the precursors for 30 s, withdrawn at 1 mm min−1, transferred into a high-grade glazed porcelain crucible with cover, and dried for 30 min at 80 °C; these processes were repeated three times. The dried samples were preheated in static air for 1 h at 200 °C and further calcined for 2 h at 325 °C.

Pr0.5Ba0.5CoO3-δ (PBC) powder was synthesised by using a combined EDTA-citrate complexing sol-gel method using Pr(NO3)3⋅6H2O, Ba(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O), and Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) as the metal precursors. Stoichiometric amounts of these metal nitrates were weighed and dissolved into de-ionised water. Then, citric acid, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and ammonia (NH3⋅H2O) solution were added under stirring to form an aqueous solution with pH of around 10. The molar ratio of total metal ions: EDTA: citric acid was 1:1:2. The mixed solution was heated at 80 °C under stirring to form a viscous gel. Afterwards, the precursor was decomposed in an oven at 300 °C, followed by calcination at 1050 °C for 5 h in air to crystallise the perovskite structure on the final powder.

Catalyst Preparation: Using a modified Pechini method, Ca3Co4O9 nanoplates were prepared. Calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (1.5 mmol) and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (2 mmol) were added into water (40 mL). Citric acid and urea were then added. After vigorous stirring (2 h) the mixture was placed in a Petri dish and rested overnight at 80 °C giving a gel. This was first decomposed for 12 h at 160 °C, followed by annealment at 700 °C for another 12 h under high purity air. Mn, Fe, and Cu-doped Ca3Co4O9 were synthesized using a similar method, in which manganese acetate, iron acetate, and anhydrous cupric acetate were used instead of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate.

The NiFe-CS was synthesized by a modified Pechini method and by thermal treatment in an oxidizing and a reducing atmosphere. The proper molar quantities of nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) and iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) were dissolved into distilled water containing citric acid (99.5%, Kanto chemical Co., Inc.) as the chelating agent. The as-obtained mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 7 h to produce a gel, which was then heated in air at 400 °C for 1 h to produce the char containing nickel-iron oxide. The as-prepared char powders were uniaxially pressed into a pellet at 1370 MPa, followed by thermal treatment under oxidizing conditions (400 °C, 1 h, ◂...▸◂◽.▸PO2≈0.21 atm) and in a reducing atmosphere (450 °C, 3 h, ◂...▸◂◽.▸PO2≈◂◽˙▸10−33 atm).

The perovskite oxides samples were prepared by sol-gel method. The stoichiometric amount of barium nitrate, strontium nitrate, cobalt(II) nitrate, and iron(III) nitrate were dissolved into deionized water. Then, EDTA (C10H16N2O8) and citric acid (C6H8O7) were added to the mixed solution sequentially. Finally, 28% ammonia water was transferred into the magnetically stirred mixture solution and the pH of the solution was adjusted to ~9. The molar ratio of metal ions : EDTA : citric acid is 1 : 1 : 1.5. Subsequently, the mixed solution was stirred and heated on a magnetic heater, and the temperature was kept at 240 °C until gelation occurred. Then, the gelled precursor was placed in a high temperature drying box and kept at a temperature of 220 °C for 6 h. The baked gel precursor was ground in an agate mortar. The solid precursor was sufficiently ground in an agate mortar to get solid precursor. Finally, the precursor materials were calcined in high temperature furnace at 900 °C for 10 h, and the calcined products were subjected to secondary grinding. Here, (Ba0.5Sr0.5)1–xCo0.8Fe0.2O3–δ (x = 0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1) are denoted as BSCF, BSCF-0.98, BSCF-0.95, and BSCF-0.9, respectively.

Fe nanoparticle precursors were synthesized by using a solution reduction method. Briefly, FeCl2∙4H2O was dissolved in deionized water to form 0.5 m Fe2+ solution. An amount of 1 m NaBH4 solution was configured in the sodium hydroxide solution with pH 11–13. Then, the NaBH4 solution was dropwise added into the Fe2+ solution under magnetic stirring for 2 h. The mixture solution was filtrated and then dried at 80 °C for 24 h in vacuum. After that, Fe nanoparticle precursors (100 mg) were placed in quartz boat and transferred to a tube furnace for being pyrolyzed in an atmosphere of flowing NH3 (150 mL min–1) at 500–650 °C for 10 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min–1. The resulting black powder product was collected for use.

LaCo1−xFexO3 NPs were prepared through a sol-gel method. In a typical synthesis, stoichiometric amounts of La(NO3)3 · 6H2O (3.75 mmol), Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O (3.75 mmol), and urea (15.0 mmol) were dissolved in a mixture solution of deionized water (30 mL) and nitric acid (3 mL) to obtain a homogeneous solution, followed by the addition of citric acid (15.0 mmol). Then the above solution was heated at 80 °C under stirring until a gel was generated and further dried at 180 °C for 12 h. The precursor powders were further annealed at 650 °C for 6 h in the air to produce LaCoO3 NPs. The LaCo1-xFexO3 (x = 0.25 and 0.75) were also prepared by using Fe(NO3)3 · 9H2O to substitute Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O with an equal molar amount.

The Ba2BixSc0.2Co1.8−xO6−δ (x=0.1, 0.2, 0.3, denoted as BBSC0.1, BBSC0.2, and BBSC0.3, respectively) catalysts were synthesized by a standard combined EDTA-CA complexing sol-gel method (EDTA=ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, CA=citric acid). Stoichiometric amounts of Ba(NO3)3, Bi(NO3)3⋅5 H2O, Sc2O3, and Co(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (all analytical grade, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) were mixed in deionized water at 90 °C. The required amount of HNO3 was added to dissolve the Bi(NO3)3⋅5 H2O. EDTA and CA, serving as complexing agents, were added at a molar ratio of 1:1:2 for the total metal ions to EDTA and CA. For complete complexation, NH3 aqueous solution was added while heating at 90 °C under continuous stirring, until a transparent gel was obtained. The gel was heated at 250 °C for 5 h in an oven to form the precursor. Finally, the precursor was calcined at 1050 °C for 5 h in air. The same synthesis method was applied to BSCF and PBC; however, the calcination temperature was 1000 °C. The cooled powder was ground by hand using an agate mortar and pestle. Commercial IrO2 was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Shanghai, China.

The single/double perovskite (SP/DP) system with the nominal composition Sr2Fe0.2Co0.8Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ, double perovskite Sr2Fe0.8Co0.2Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ, and single perovskite SrFe0.5Co0.5O3−δ were synthesized by a sol-gel method. Taking Sr2Fe0.2Co0.8Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ (10 mmol) as an example, Sr(NO3)2 (4.2326 g), Fe(NO3)3⋅9 H2O (3.2320 g), Co(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (1.7462 g), (NH4)Mo7O24⋅4 H2O (1.1476 g), and Ni(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (1.4540 g) (all analytical grade from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in deionized water with the assistance of citric acid (CA, 16.8 g) and C10H16N2O8 (EDTA, 11.6 g). An aqueous NH3 solution was added to adjust the pH to ≈6. A transparent gel was obtained under continued magnetic stirring at ≈90 °C on a hotplate. Next, the gel was heated to 200 °C for 5 h to form a precursor powder. The precursor was calcined in air at 1100 °C for 10 h. SrFe0.5Co0.5O3−δ was calcined at 900 °C for 5 h in air.

5 mg of Ni NPs dispersed in 20 ml of THF was added into 20 ml of NMP solution of 10 mg graphene (0.5 mg/ml) and the mixture was sonicated for 1 h. 50 ml ethanol was added and the precipitate was separated from the solvents by centrifuging for 15 min at 4000 rpm and dried under ambient conditions for further use.

Synthesis of Solid Cu2O Nanocrystals: A 0.17 g CuCl2·2H2O was first dissolved in deionized water (100 mL) at room temperature, yielding a light-blue transparent solution. Then 10 mL 2 m NaOH was added to the solution. After 30 min stirring at the temperature of 30 °C, 10 mL 0.6 m ascorbic acid was introduced dropwisely into the system. With another 30 min agitation, a brilliant yellow precipitate was obtained. The precipitate was collected and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times by centrifugation, then dried at 60 °C overnight. The nanostructures of the Cu2O sample are shown in Figure S1, Supporting Information.

Firstly, the carbon cloth (2 × 2 cm) was ultrasonically washed with deionized water and ethanol, then be treated with KOH and hydrochloric acid reported previously . To enhance the interaction between active materials and carbon cloth, the treated carbon cloth (CC) was immersed in a concentrated HNO3 solution at 80 ℃ for 2 h, and then washed with deionized water to neutrality and dried for standby application. Then, the Co-S/CC was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Dissolving 0.1 M Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.6 M CO(NH2)2, and 0.1 M CH4N2S in 15 mL of a mixed solution involving deionized water and ethanol (Vwater/Vethanol = 4:1)with vigorous stirring until obtained clear solution. The aforementioned mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, a piece of CC (1 cm * 2 cm) was placed in the above autoclave and kept at 180 ℃ for 18 h. After being cooled down to room temperature naturally, the CC turned to a pitaya color. Then, the as-prepared sample was rinsed with deionized water several times and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ˚C for 12 h. Finally, the sample was placed in a quartz boat and calcined at 350 ℃ for 3 h with 3 ℃ min−1 under N2. The synthesized material is recorded as Co-S/CC.

Synthesis of Amorphous Ni–Co Double Hydroxide Nanoparticles: A 85 mg of NiCl2·6H2O and CoCl2·6H2O mixture (mass ratio of 3:7) was added into the 100 mL ethanol/water mixed solvent (volume ratio = 1: 1) with presence of 3.333 g polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) (Mw = 30 000). After stirring for 10 min, 10 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (1 m) was added dropwise into the above mixture. Then the reaction was carried out at room temperature for about 3 h. Eventually, amorphous Ni–Co hydroxides nanoparticles were formed.

For the calculations of mass activity and specific activity, reported method was followed. Details of the calculations of mass activity and specific activity are shown below. Mass activity value (A/g) was calculated from the catalyst loading and the observed current density (mA/cm2geo) at potential 1.7 V vs. RHE. Specific activity (mA/cm2ECSA) was calculated by normalizing the current at a fixed potential (1.7 V) by the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA).Mass activity = observed current density at a fixed potential/catalyst loadingSpecific Activity = observed current at a fixed potential/ECSA

Tafel plots taken between 1.4 and 1.45 V vs RHE, Figure 8, show similar gradients (between 40 and 50 mV dec–1) and thus suggest that all catalysts operate through the same reaction mechanism. The values of Tafel slope reported in the literature for RuO2 can vary significantly between different samples, being dependent on the surface area and crystallinity of the materials; for example, Iwakura et al. showed that the value measured for films of RuO2 increased from 40 to 70 mV dec–1 with an increase in annealing temperature of the electrocatalyst.  Typically, however, a value of ∼40 mV dec–1 is found for many RuO2 samples reported in the literature.  The value of the Tafel slope has been used to infer mechanistic information about the OER process in oxides: at 25 °C, values of ∼120 mV dec–1 have been interpreted as arising the case when the first electron transfer step in a sequential reaction is rate-determining, values of ∼40 mV dec–1 if a second electron transfer step is rate-determining, and intermediate values of ∼60 mV dec–1 when the rate-determining step involves a chemical step subsequent to the first electron transfer.  Note that at 60 °C, as in the experiments here, this gradient is expected to be higher, so that a second electron transfer step as rate-determining would give a Tafel slope of ∼45 mV dec–1, in line with our observations.

The OER and HER performances of all catalysts in this study were evaluated separately in standard three-electrode cells with a 1 M KOH electrolyte. iR correction was applied to all measurements. The OER overpotential, a key performance indicator for OER electrocatalysis, was defined as the potential difference from 1.23 V (vs RHE) which is required to support a current density of 50 mA cm–2 (η 50). At this low current density, the contribution from the Ni(II)/Ni(III) redox reaction at ∼1.4 V is relatively minor. Figure 3a shows the outstanding OER activity of Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF, with η 50 as low as 250 mV which compares well with the rest of the catalysts (η 50 = 290 mV for Fe-free NiS-(BO)/NF, η 50 of 368 mV for undoped NiS/NF, and η 50 = 417 mV for the NF substrate). The activity of Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF also surpasses that of the reference IrO2/NF catalyst (η 50 = 317 mV). The other important performance indicator, the OER Tafel slope (A), was calculated from the following equation(2)where η is the measured overpotential, j the current density, and j0 is the exchange current density. A small Tafel slope is indicative of favorable OER kinetics. The OER Tafel slope was the lowest from Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF (54.5 mV dec–1, Figure 3b), followed by IrO2/NF (63.2 mV dec–1), NiS-(BO)/NF (65.9 mV dec–1), NiS/NF (88.0 mV dec–1), and NF (117.9 mV dec–1). A comparison of OER η 50 and the Tafel slope with current OER catalysts in 1 M KOH (Table 2) clearly places the Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF catalyst as among the top of the PGM alternatives.

The HER activity of the catalysts was assessed likewise. Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF again emerged as having the highest HER activity (Figure 3c), where its overpotential of 200 mV to support 50 mA cm–2 of current density (defined as η 50-HER) is closer to that of the reference Pt/C/NF catalyst (126 mV) than NiS-(BO)/NF (272 mV), NiS/NF (339 mV), and NF (440 mV). In addition, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF also showed favorable HER kinetics by having a Tafel slope comparable to that of Pt/C/NF (57.9 and 40.1 mV dec–1, respectively, in Figure 3d), which is much smaller than those of NiS-(BO)/NF (77.8 mV dec–1), NiS/NF (101.2 mV dec–1), and NF (134.6 mV dec–1). Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF is also one of the best of transition-metal-based HER catalysts based on our literature survey conducted to date (Table 3). In summary, the concurrent cation and anion doping of the Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF catalyst has integrated remarkable OER and good HER activities in a common catalyst, which ranks among the best of the non-PGM research catalysts developed to date.

Since high surface area contributes to high catalytic activity,  the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) of NiS/NF, NiS-(BO)/NF, and Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF were also estimated from electrochemical capacitance measurements and compared. The measurements were taken in the −0.5–0.5 V voltage window where no Faradaic process occurred.  Based on the cyclic voltammograms recorded at different scan rates, CDL was calculated from the slope of a linear plot of double-layer capacitive current (taken as the midpoint current from the voltammogram) against scan rate (Figure 4a–d). ECSA was then calculated from CDL by the following equation(3)where CS is the capacitance of an ideal planar surface with few irregularities. A value of 25 μF cm–2 is typical for the 1 M KOH electrolyte.  The ECSAs calculated as such were 149.6, 154.4, and 158 cm2 for NiS/NF, NiS-(BO)/NF, and Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF, respectively. These values are high among the transition metal catalysts.  The surface area contribution to the measured activities was discounted by normalizing the measured activities of the catalysts by their ECSAs (Figure 4e and f). It was found that the catalysts still displayed the same trend of activity differences. The good OER and HER performance of Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF was therefore chemical in origin and primarily due to the enhancement of the intrinsic activity of Ni3S2 by anion and cation doping.

The above understanding highlights the possibility of catalyst activity tuning via catalyst composition optimization. The cation and anion dopant contents of NiS were therefore varied by adjusting the amounts of borate and Fe precursors in the synthesis, and the performance of the resulting catalysts in OER and HER was measured and compared. Two series of catalysts were prepared—one by fixing the amount of the Fe precursor salt (iron(II) sulfate, 1.5 mmol) in the synthesis and varying only the borate content (Fe-Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B0.5, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B1, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B1.5, and Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B2 where the number indicates the borate feed content in mmol) and the other by fixing the borate feed content (1.5 mmol) and varying the Fe precursor content in the synthesis (Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-Fe0, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-Fe0.5, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-Fe1, Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-Fe1.5, and Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-Fe2, where the number indicates the Fe feed content in mmol). The OER and HER polarization curves of these catalysts are shown in Figure 5a and b, based on which the overpotentials for OER, HER, and total water splitting at 50 mA cm–2 (η 50-OER, η 50-HER, and η 50 (calculated as the sum of η 50-OER and η 50-HER)) were calculated and compared (Figure 5c). The η 50 of Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B0.5 was 555 mV, which decreased to 484 mV for Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B1 and 450 mV for Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B1.5.

The trend was reversed with further increases of the borate feed content, resulting in a η 50 of 524 mV in the case of Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF-B2. The “near optimal” anion (borate) doping in this study was therefore a B/S atomic ratio of 39.6 from the NiS-(BO)/NF-B1.5 catalyst. An excess of borate content is believed to reduce the number of Ni active sites and increase the steric hindrance of the borate groups crowding on the catalyst surface. The “optimal” cation composition was also determined from similar OER and HER measurements. The results showed that a Fe/Ni atomic ratio of 18.9 provided the “near optimal” Fe-doping content for total water splitting (Figure 6).

Tafel analysis is often used to deduce the rate-determining step (RDS) in OER. A Tafel slope of ∼40 mV dec–1, for example, suggests the formation of OOHads (step III) as the RDS. Similarly, a Tafel slope of ∼60 mV dec–1 suggests the formation of Oads (step II) as the RDS,  and a Tafel slope greater than 120 mV dec–1 suggests the formation of OHads (step I) as the RDS.  Therefore, OER Tafel slopes of NiS/NF, NiS-(BO)/NF, and Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF of 88.0, 65.9, and 54.5 mV dec–1, respectively, are indicative of Oads formation (step II) as the RDS, and borate and Fe doping has helped promote Oads formation (step II). In our last study, we proposed that three-coordinated borate could transform into four-coordinated borate via reactions with hydroxyl ions (the Lewis base) in an alkaline electrolyte. This would facilitate the proton transfer from adsorbed OH to form Oads on the catalytic metal center.  Since Fe-NiS-(BO)/NF contained both Fe(II) and Fe(III) which could withdraw electrons from Ni, OHads on Ni was rendered less stable, thus lowering the activation energy of Oads formation (step II).

To get further insight into the reaction kinetics, a Tafel plot for NiSxPy–O is derived from corresponding overpotential regions with about 100% O2 Faradaic efficiency in 1.0 M KOH. As shown in Figure 4c, the Tafel slope of 95.0 mV dec–1 for NiSxPy–O is much lower than that of 151.2 mV dec–1 for Ni3S2–O and 112.7 mV dec–1 for Ni2P–O, implying favorable OER kinetics. Moreover, the Tafel plot is also derived from the low overpotential region from 180 to 240 mV based on fitted J–V curves (Figure S15), and similar data indicate that it is feasible to calculate the Tafel slope from the fitted J–V curve corrected by Faradaic efficiency. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to further evaluate the electron transfer kinetics across the electrode (Figure 4d). The Nyquist plots reveal a much smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct) for NiSxPy–O than that for Ni3S2–O and Ni2P–O, which may result from the fast reaction kinetics for electrocatalytic water oxidation or larger surface area.

In summary, a metallic nickel sulfide/phosphide hybrid (NiSxPy) with hierarchical nanostructures where 1D nanowires are grown on 1D nanorods has been successfully developed as a promising electrocatalyst to drive oxygen evolution. The NiSxPy–O catalyst features extraordinary activity toward OER, only needing an overpotential of 192 mV (with 100% iR compensation; this value should be 200 mV without compensation) in 1.0 M KOH to achieve the O2 partial current density of 10 mA cm–2. The high performance could be attributed to the synergistic effects resulting from 1D/1D hierarchical nanostructures and intrinsically improved OER activity. The unique nanostructures favor the exposure of NiOOH species in situ-generated during the OER process as active sites. At the same time, the conductive 1D/1D nanostructures combined with metallic features of hybrid NiSxPy supply an electron highway, guaranteeing fast charge transportation efficiency during electrolysis. This work might provide new insights to rationally design metallic precatalysts integrated with hierarchical nanostructures toward efficient OER electrocatalysis in different media, which could be used for renewable energy conversion and storage applications.

Electrochemical water splitting to produce hydrogen can meet growing energy needs while balancing the impact of severe fossil fuel consumption. To date, it is still greatly desirable to obtain bifunctional and precious metal-free electrocatalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) simultaneously during overall water splitting toward actual applications. Herein, we develop a cocrystallized strategy to obtain a Co/Ni-based mixed metal–organic nanosheet array (NiCo-9AC-AD) on a nickel foam (NF) substrate with controlled composition and morphology as a bifunctional electrocatalyst. The optimized Ni0.3Co0.7-9AC-AD/NF reveals efficient electrocatalytical activity and very low overpotentials of 350 mV for OER to attain a current density value of 100 mA cm–2 and 143 mV for HER at 10 mA cm–2. This is due to the synergistic effect of Ni/Co double catalytical active sites and ultrathin 2D nanosheet topology. More importantly, as an exceptionally active and stable electrocatalyst, the NiCo-9AC-AD nanosheet array requires merely 1.56 V to deliver a current density value of 10 mA cm–2 in an overall water-splitting application. This work thus develops a simple approach to obtain a highly active cocrystallized metal–organic hybrid for both OER and HER, which may open the way to rational design of similar Ni/Co-based supramolecular systems toward electrochemical and energy storage applications.

In this work, (NiFe)S2 nanoparticles grown on GN (abbr. (NiFe)S2-GN) are fabricated by a sulfidation of the NiFe LDH-GO composites. Under optimized experimental condition, the as-prepared (NiFe)S2-GN-0.2 exhibits superior OER performance with an overpotential of 320 mV to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH, and the Tafel slope of 61 mV/dec is smaller than the Tafel slope (68 mV/dec) of commercial RuO2.

The KOH electrolyte was saturated with N2 before the electrochemical test for HER. As indicated by the LSV curves with iR compensation (Fig. 6a, Fig. S28-S31, Table S4), among the samples prepared with different components and structure as well as synthesis parameters, the NCT@CoP@MoS2 sample with the best OER performance also showed the highest HER activity, although it was still lower than 20 wt% Pt/C. For example, the NCT@CoP@MoS2 electrode showed the lowest η 10 value of 195 mV than the as-prepared catalysts of MoS2 (667 mV), NCT (465 mV), CNT@CoP (235 mV), and NCT@CoP (208 mV). For HER, CoP in the catalysts also played a pivotal role. Although the facilitating roles of MoS2 coating and the ZIF-8 inner tube derived NCT were not as obvious as in OER, the enhancement of HER performance can still be observed. Besides, NCT@CoP@MoS2 showed the lowest Tafel value of 74 mV·dec−1 among the as-prepared catalysts, indicating the high HER kinetics of NCT@CoP@MoS2 for HER (Fig. 6b). Also, NCT@CoP@MoS2 had the smallest semicircle diameter among the contrast samples, thus a favorable Rct for HER (Fig. 6c). The ADT was measured from −1.1 to −1.4 V and the overpotential of NCT@CoP@MoS2 increased only 19 mV after 5000 CV cycles (Fig. 6d). Moreover, there was no obvious morphology or component change after the cycling test (Fig. S32-S34). The above results confirmed that the NCT@CoP@MoS2 also delivered high electrochemical activity and durability for HER.

Developing highly efficient, stable, and inexpensive electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is an ongoing challenge due to the high energy barrier imposed by the OER during electrochemical water splitting. Herein, we report one-dimensional iron-cobalt oxide (FeCo2O4) nanorod arrays supported on nickel foam (NF) synthesized via a simple chemical bath deposition (CBD) method to accelerate OER. Consequently, the FeCo2O4 nanorods exhibit significantly increased OER activity in alkaline solutions while maintaining high stability and a low OER overpotential of 290 mV at 25 mA cm−2. The improved OER performance is attributed to the electrode's enhanced intrinsic electrocatalytic activity, which is due to the synergetic effect of Fe and Co, a large number of active sites, and a low charge transfer resistance.

In this work, one-dimensional NiHPO4 nanotubes are successfully fabricated on nickel foam by hydrothermal reaction, in which a dissolution equilibrium between phosphates is controlled by tuning the proportion of the mixed solvent and amounts of KOH. As the dissolution equilibrium is broken, the morphology of NiHPO4 transfers from solid nanowires to hollow nanotubes. The resulting 1D NiHPO4 nanotubes exhibit good electrocatalytic activity and stability in oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Notably, a water-splitting voltage of 1.62 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 is obtained in an electrolyzer setup assembled using 1D NiHPO4 nanotubes as cathode and anode, demonstrating NiHPO4 nanotubes are promising catalysts for overall water splitting. Moreover, the revealed mechanism of forming tube morphology can be extended to fabricate other metal phosphates with hollow structures.

Herein, we have developed a facile process of synthesizing N and O surface-terminated 2D molybdenum carbide nanomeshes (Mo2CTx NMs) and assembling them into 3D microflowers (Mo2CTx MFs) by one-step pyrolysis of Mo/Zn bimetallic imidazolate frameworks. When used as an oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalyst, the Mo2CTx NMs thus derived exhibit outstanding catalytic activity with an overpotential of 180 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2. This enables Mo2CTx NMs to become one of the best OER electrocatalysts ever reported, with the desired stability in alkaline environment which is a major challenge for most of the non-oxide/hydroxide based electrocatalyts. Additionally, the Mo2CTx MFs can catalyze the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and act as bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting which can attain a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at 1.7 V. Mo LIII-edge X-ray near-edge absorption studies combined with theoretical calculations imply that surface-terminated oxygen is crucial in activating the outstanding OER performance, whereas the top Mo atomic sites on the surface contribute to excellent HER performance.

In summary, the 2D Fe-CoP/CoO lateral heterostructure is synthesized via an instantaneous and scalable molten alkali-assisted method with reasonable components and active interfaces exposed to surface rather than bulk, which promises it with increased charge transfer densities, fast reaction kinetics as well as fully contact between Fe-CoP/CoO and electrolyte. Owning to the advantages above, the 2D Fe-CoP/CoO lateral heterostructure exhibits a remarkable catalytic activity with the overpotential of 219 mV at 10 mA cm-2 towards OER, compared to most of the reported electrocatalysts. Moreover, DFT calculation reveals that oxygen in CoP/CoO interfaces decreases the Gibbs free energy of ΔG2 from 1.75 to 1.40 eV and significantly changed the rate-determining step from step 2 (ΔG (*O)) to step 3 (ΔG (*OOH)) compared to pure CoP, benefitting water oxidation process. Incorporation of Fe further optimizes the Gibbs free energy of the rate-determining step from 1.71 to 1.64 eV due to the homogenous metal synergistic effect, inducing OER much easier compared to pure CoP/CoO. At last, our results show that the engineering the interfaces exposed to the catalyst surface is a favorable direction for developing low-cost and high-activity hybrid electrocatalysts, which have promising applications for various energy storages and conversions.

Herein, we have introduced a templated growth strategy to synthesize iron-doped nickel MOF nanosheets on NF (Fe-Ni MOF NSs/NF) through a facile one-step solvothermal method . Only one kind of metal salt precursor was used in the precursor solution, which might be propitious to the simple and inexpensive preparation of the OER electrocatalyst. The binder-free Fe-Ni MOF NSs/NF exhibits remarkable electrocatalytic performance, requiring small overpotentials of 258 and 270 mV to achieve the current densities of 50 and 100 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH solution. What is more, it is found that both the nanosheet morphology and the chemical structure of the catalyst contribute to its superior and durable OER performance.

Development of cost-effective, efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is a challenging issue as OER has sluggish reaction kinetics due to transfer of multi-electrons. In this study, a new strategy has been developed for the synthesis of 2D nanostructures of CoFe2O4 and NiFe2O4 following a wet-chemical route followed by calcination. Following this method nanoplates of CoFe2O4 and nanosheets of NiFe2O4 have been successfully synthesized. These interconnected 2D structures are very efficient for oxygen evolution reaction and it is observed that CoFe2O4 nanoplates and NiFe2O4 nanosheets are catalytically more active compared to nanocubes and nanobipyramids of CoFe2O4 and NiFe2O4. CoFe2O4 nanoplates require only 1.64 V vs. RHE for generating current density of 10 mA/cm2 whereas nanocubes of CoFe2O4 require 1.68 V vs. RHE. Similarly, NiFe2O4 nanosheets require 1.69 V to generate current density 10 mA/cm2 and NiFe2O4 nanobipyramids require 1.77 V vs. RHE to engender 10 mA/cm2 current density. 2D sheet or Plate-like structure with more exposed surface atoms faces more electrolyte to adsorb and react which results in higher electrocatalytic activity.

To get insight into the rate-determining step during the OER process under these conditions, Tafel analysis was conducted (Fig. 2c). It's known that in alkaline solutions oxygen evolution undergoes a multistep reaction process of converting OH− to O2. If the first electron transfer process of OER is the rate-determining step, the corresponding Tafel slope is 120 mV dec−1; if the second chemical reaction process is the rate-limiting step, the relevant Tafel slope is 60 mV dec−1. Differently, if the electron–proton reaction is the rate-limiting step, the Tafel slope is 40 mV dec−1. In our case, the estimated Tafel slope value of BP NSs is much higher than 120.0 mV dec−1, suggesting that the electron transport process is the rate-determining step for the OER on the BP NSs. The Tafel slope value of single CNT is 63.23 mV dec−1, indicating chemical reaction process is the rate-limiting step. As for the best BP/CNT-(1:2), it shows the Tafel slope value of 59.29 mV dec−1, telling that the chemical reaction process is the rate-determining step instead of electron transfer process and electron transfer is visibly accelerated after introduction of CNT to BP NSs.

3D flower-like Co3O4/N–C fabricated by the calcination of cobalt hydroxide/polypyrrole composite offers a small Tafel slope (33 mV dec–1) and a low overpotential (120 mV) in KOH (1.0 M) for oxygen evolution reaction.

In contrast to Ni, it was possible to record reproducible log i(E) plots for the OER at Co anodes, that had received no pre-treatment beyond polishing. Such plots are presented for several electrolyte concentrations in Fig. 5. It is immediately obvious that there is a difficulty regarding the extraction of the reaction order from this data, namely, the fact that the Tafel slope increases from 38 mV dec−1 in 1.0 M NaOH to 46 mV dec−1 in 5 M solution. Formally, this implies a change in either OER mechanism or in the rate determining step (RDS) within a given pathway, thus rendering as meaningless any derived value of mOH-. This difficulty was overcome by subjecting the Co electrode to a pre-treatment involving a 5 min cathodic reduction at –1.15 V in 1 M NaOH, followed by a single potential cycle at 40 mV s−1 between –1.22 and 0.6 V in the same solution. Steady state polarisation curves for various NaOH test solutions, recorded subsequent to such pre-treatment, are presented in Fig. 6. With the observation of Tafel slopes of ∼46 mV dec−1 across the examined range of electrolyte concentration, reaction order plots were constructed for several of the associated values of potential. Typical of these, is the plot for E = 0.6 V, which is included as an inset in Fig. 6 and indicates an mOH- value of the order of unity.

HER activities of Cu(OH)2/CF, CoNiCu-MOF@Cu2O/CF, and CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF electrodes were compared with those of Pt/C/CF electrodes. As displayed in Fig. 6a, the Cu(OH)2/CF, CoNiCu-MOF@Cu2O/CF, and CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF electrodes need 523, 298, and 268 mV overpotentials at 100 mA cm−2 current density, respectively (Fig. 6b). Like with OER test results, CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF shows higher HER performance than Cu(OH)2/CF and CoNiCu-MOF@Cu2O/CF, also better than some similar catalysts. As expected, the commercial Pt/C immobilized on CF yields the highest HER activity among all prepared samples, reaching 100 mA cm−2 with an overpotential of only 108 mV. Furthermore, the Tafel slopes of these samples were obtained by fitting the steady polarization curves (Fig. 6c). CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF gives the lowest Tafel slope of 88.4 mV dec−1, better than 95.7 mV dec-1 of commercial Pt/C catalyst, indicating its fast kinetics and excellent HER intrinsic activity. Moreover, the CoNiCu-LDH@CuO/CF electrode exhibits a low charge transfer resistance (Fig. 6d).

We also confirmed the OER activity of NiFe alloy treated with different reduction temperatures. Firstly, the performance was enhanced with respect to an increase in reduction temperature up to 500 °C, as shown in Fig. S5. However, the higher temperature caused a serious agglomeration of the NiFe alloy particles (Fig. S2), leading to lower activity for the OER. Consequently, the optimal reduction temperature was found to be 500 °C. To gain a greater understanding of OER activity, OER catalytic kinetics in an alkaline solution were assessed using Tafel plots, as shown in Fig. 5b, where the NiFe alloy exhibits a significantly lower Tafel slope (51.9 mV dec−1) compared to those of Ni (71.7 mV dec−1) and Fe (103.7 mV dec−1). The Tafel slope of the NiFe alloy is also competitive with those of previously reported highly active alkaline OER catalysts, indicating that these particles have favorable reaction kinetics. A detailed comparison of various highly active electrocatalysts for OERs is provided in Table S1, confirming the excellent electrochemical performance of the synthesized NiFe alloy.

Also, we carried out CP test on Ni0.95|Mn0.05O/CNT with the best performance at 10 mA cm−2, and the test results are shown in Fig. 3g. It can be seen that the voltage is almost unchanged after continuous reaction for 20 h in 1 M KOH, indicating that the catalyst has excellent stability. In summary, Nix|Mn1-xO heterostructure can promote the OER catalytic performance, which is consistent to the previously reported literature . And our experiment results show that the Ni0.95|Mn0.05O/CNT has the best OER catalytic performance.

The electrocatalytic activity of NixFe1−xSe2-DO, NiFe LDH, NiSe2-DO, NiSe-DO and Ni foam (NF) towards OER in 1 M KOH oxidation was measured and compared (see Methods for details). As shown in Fig. 6a, NixFe1−xSe2-DO is the best catalyst among the five compounds, giving much higher current density (J) at the same overpotential (η). To reach J=10 mA cm−2, NixFe1−xSe2-DO required an overpotential of only 195 mV, which was 49, 46, 58 and 96 mV less than that of NiFe LDH, NiSe2-DO, NiSe-DO and NF, respectively (Fig. 6b). The current density at η=250 mV was 262 mA cm−2, which was 16-, 18-, 29- and 262-fold higher than those of NiFe LDH, NiSe2-DO, NiSe-DO and NF, respectively (Fig. 6c). In fact, the activity of NixFe1−xSe2-DO is superior to other state-of-the-art catalysts (Supplementary Table 1). NiFe LDH has been regarded as the most active OER catalyst in alkaline conditions. Lu et al. reported the overpotential of ∼256 mV for 10 mA cm−2 by NiFe LDH grown on Ni foam. The catalytic activity of NiFe LDH was further improved by applying an improved electrosynthesis method. However, an overpotential of 224 mV for 10 mA cm−2 was still required.

The activity of NixFe1−xSe2-DO compares favourably even to the best hybrid catalysts made of NiFeOx and carbon nanomateirals. Ma et al. reported that the NiFe hydroxide/graphene superlattice reached 10 mA cm−2 at an overpotential of 210 mV. Hou et al. reported that exfoliated graphene/Co0.85Se/NiFe LDH composites catalysed OER with an overpotential of 203 mV for 10 mA cm−2. The most active hybrid catalyst, r-GO/NiFe LDH, was reported by by Long et al., which gave 10 mA cm−2 at an overpotential of 195 mV. It was proposed that the activity of these hybrid materials originated from a synergetic effect between NiFeOx and carbon nanomaterials (CNT, r-GO and carbon quantum dots). Thus, our NixFe1−xSe2-DO is until now the most active single-phase catalyst, and even higher activity might be achieved by coupling it to carbon nanomaterials. The influence of Ni:Fe atomic ratio on the OER activity was probed. The highest activity was obtained at x=0.8, while the samples with x=0.9 and 0.67 exhibited only modestly lower activity (Supplementary Fig. 5). This result is consistent with previous reports that that the activity of NiFeOx varied only slightly when the Fe:Ni ratio was changed from 0.1 and 0.55 (refs , ).

By plotting overpotential against log (J), the kinetic parameters of OER by the five catalysts were calculated (Fig. 6d). The NixFe1−xSe2-DO has a Tafel slope of about 28 mV dec−1, close to that of NiFe LDH (32 mV dec−1), but much smaller than the other catalysts (40–54 mV dec−1). We further compared the electrochemical surface area (ECSA), estimated from their double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of NixFe1−xSe2-DO and NiFe LDH. The ECSA of NixFe1−xSe2-DO is 2.4-fold of the ECSA of NiFe LDH (Fig. 6e) that does not account for the 16-fold higher catalytic activity of the former. We attribute the superior activity of NixFe1−xSe2-DO to its desirable nanostructure. As shown in Fig. 4, NixFe1−xSe2-DO nanoparticles are made of ultrathin nanosheets. If the edges of NiFeOx are the active sites, then a higher number of active sites is expected on NixFe1−xSe2-DO than other forms of NiFeOx. To provide additional support for this hypothesis, the microstructures of NixFe1−xSe2-DO was compared with NiFe LDH, one of the most active forms of NiFeOx (refs , ).

The stability of OER catalysed by the NixFe1−xSe2-DO electrode was tested at a constant current density J of 10 mA cm−2 for 24 h. Figure 6f shows that after an activation period of 0.5 h, the overpotential remained at ∼195 mV during 24 h. The Faradaic efficiency of OER was determined using a fluorescence O2 probe. A quantitative yield was found quantitative during a 6.5 h electrolysis experiment (inset in Fig. 6f).

Figure 5a illustrates that the electrocatalytic activity of Ni3Fe1−xVx depends largely on the co-doping level of Fe and V atoms. Among the as-prepared Fe- or/and V-doped Ni-based binary and ternary catalysts, Ni3Fe0.5V0.5 exhibits the best OER performance, with low overpotentials of 264 and 291 mV to achieve 100 and 500 mA cm−2 current density, respectively (Supplementary Fig. 18). The LSV of Ni3Fe0.5V0.5, scanning from positive to negative direction to exclude the influence of the Ni2+/Ni3+ oxidation event on the catalytic current, shows that only 200 mV overpotential is required to attain 10 mA cm−2 current density. The OER performance of Ni3Fe0.5V0.5 is on a par with or even surpasses that of the first-class earth-abundant catalysts reported to date (Supplementary Table 7).

In order to illustrate the advantages of the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrocatalyst, the OER activity of Au/HPG, NiCo2O4/HPG and Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG is evaluated through linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH with a sweep rate of 0.001 V s−1 as shown in Fig. 5a. The sum of loading for Au and NiCo2O4 on the electrodes was accurately controlled at 0.1 mg cm−2. The onset potential (Eonset) of OER is 0.589 V on the Au/HPG electrode, 0.520 V on the NiCo2O4/HPG electrode and 0.512 V on the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrode. The value of Eonset on the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrode is 8 and 77 mV lower than that on the NiCo2O4/HPG and Au/HPG electrodes. When the value of Eonset is lower, the OER happens easier. OER has the lowest value of Eonset on the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrode, so OER happens the most easily on the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrode. The values of current density at 0.7 V (j0.7V) are 1.4, 6.8 and 9.1 mA cm−2 on the Au/HPG, NiCo2O4/HPG and Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrodes.

The Au is poorly active for OER and the NiCo2O4 as a major electrocatalyst shows a considerable activity. The current density on the NiCo2O4/HPG improves remarkably by adding a small amount of Au. There is a synergistic effect between Au and NiCo2O4 because the value of Eonset is lower and the current density is higher on the Au-NiCo2O4(wt 1:5)/HPG electrode than that on the Au/HPG and NiCo2O4/HPG electrodes. The values of Eonset and j0.7V of LSV on the Au-NiCo2O4/HPG catalysts with different weight percent of Au are compared as shown in Fig. 5b. The sum of loading for Au and NiCo2O4 on the electrodes was accurately controlled at 0.1 mg cm−2 in the Au-NiCo2O4/HPG electrode. The lowest value of Eonset is 0.512 V when weight ratio for Au to NiCo2O4 is 1: 5. The highest value of j0.7V is 9.1 mA cm−2 when weight ratio for Au to NiCo2O4 is 1: 5.

Tafel plots were derived from polarization curves to gain the kinetic parameters of the electrodes. As drawn in Fig. 5c, the p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu electrocatalyst had a Tafel slope of ~52 mV dec−1, which is close to that of IrO2 (45 mV dec−1) but much smaller than other catalysts (76–163 mV dec−1), indicating that the rate-determining step of the p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu electrocatalyst occurs near the end of the multiple-electron transfer reaction, which is commonly a sign of an efficient electrocatalyst. Further, we compared the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of each catalyst, which was obtained from the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) (Supplementary Fig. 12). It was found that the p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu catalyst exhibited the largest Cdl (10.5 mF cm−2) among all four samples, demonstrating the highest electrochemically active surface area. The polarization curves of the catalysts normalized by ECSA are displayed in Supplementary Fig. 13. Obviously, the p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu still showed the best OER activity among the catalysts. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed to demonstrate the significant electrode kinetic of the p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu. Nyquist plots were depicted in Supplementary Fig. 14 for the CuNNi3+Cu, CuNNi3, Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu and p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu electrodes. The p-Cu1−xNNi3−y/FeNiCu catalyst had a much lower charge–transfer resistance (Rct) value, corresponding to a rate of reaction that was faster than those of the other samples.

The OER electrocatalytic activity of Co9S8/N,S-rGO was performed in alkaline electrolyte (1 M KOH) in a typical three electrode configuration. All data was corrected with iR compensation. In the experiment, RuO2 nanopowders were used as benchmark catalyst. OER performance of Co9S8, N,S-rGO and Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO were also performed (Fig. 5). The overpotential to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2 is an important factor to evaluate the oxygen evolution activity of electrocatalysts. According to the linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) curves displayed in Fig. 5a, the overpotential at current density of 10 mA cm−2 for Co9S8/N,S-rGO was only 266 mV, which is much lower than that of Co9S8 (297 mV) and Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO (292 mV), and closed to that of benchmarking RuO2 (232 mV). The bare N,S-rGO possessed an overpotential of more than 462 mV even at 2 mA cm−2, suggesting that in Co9S8/N,S-rGO, Co9S8 is the main contributor for OER electrocatalytic active sites. Moreover, Co9S8/N,S-rGO with ultrathin Co9S8 nanosheets vertical growth on N,S-rGO can provide large exposed catalytic active sites, probably accounting for higher activity in comparsion to Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO. Such an overpotential is much lower than other cobalt compounds in alkaline media towards OER, such as hierarchical cobalt sulphide, O-CoS2-MoS2 heteronanosheet, CoO/N-Graphene, etc. Tafel slopes were calculated to analyze the OER kinetics of all electrocatalysts. The high OER activity of Co9S8/N,S-rGO was confirmed by the small Tafel slope (75.5 mV dec−1) in Fig. 5b, which was much lower than those of Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO (90 mV dec−1), Co9S8 (117 mV dec−1) and N,S-rGO (110 mV dec−1).

The different Tafel slope values of electrocatalysts signified the different electrochemical oxidation pathways and rate-determining steps. The N,S-rGO with the largest Tafel slope value demonstrated the intrinsically inferior oxygen evolution property. Compared with Co9S8 and Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO, Tafel slope of Co9S8/N,S-rGO showed distinct acceleration of oxygen evolution kinetics arising from the interaction between Co9S8 and N,S-rGO. The small Tafel slope values demonstrated that the high OER kinetics and low hydroxyl free radical absorption free energy of Co9S8/N,S-rGO during oxygen evolution. Figure 5c displayed the comparison of Tafel slope and potential data of current density at 10 mA cm−2. The potential of Co9S8/N,S-rGO was 1.496 V vs. RHE, smaller than Co9S8 nps/N,S-rGO, Co9S8 and N,S-rGO. To further explore the catalytic activities of Co9S8/N,S-rGO, the mass activity and specific activity were also calculated (Table 1). At overpotential of 0.27 V, the mass activity and specific activity of Co9S8/N,S-rGO were 27.03 A g−1 and 0.035 mA cm−2, respectively. These excellent performance mainly attributed to the hierarchical architecture with ultrathin Co9S8 nanosheets vertically grown on N,S-rGO which can increase the number of catalytic active sites by exposing large effective surface area and facilitate fast electron transport.

The OER activities of Ni1−xFexO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF and the reference samples were tested and compared in an O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH solution using a standard three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, Ni1−xFexOOH/NiO/NF and NiO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF catalysts exhibited higher OER activities than NiOOH/NiO/NF and the benchmark RuO2/NF, particularly at a higher current density. In comparison with the Fe-doped and F-doped samples, the (Fe, F) co-doped sample exhibited a more pronounced activity enhancement, highlighting the synergistic effect of cation–anion co-doping. Ni1−xFexO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF requires a low overpotential of only 186 mV to afford 10 mA cm−2, which is among the best results for alkaline OER catalysts (Table S3, ESI†). In addition, it was noticed that the oxidation wave corresponding to the Ni2+ → Ni3+ transformation was invisible in the Fe-containing samples, suggesting a high Fe-doping level. This is consistent with the result from XPS surface analysis, which determined a Fe/Ni/F molar ratio of around 1 : 4 : 1 for the optimal catalyst. The activity advantage of Ni1−xFexO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF over the reference samples was also manifested by the Tafel plots (Fig. 5c). Ni1−xFexO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF showed a Tafel slope of 32 mV dec−1, which was much smaller than that of Ni1−xFexOOH/NiO/NF (55 mV dec−1), NiO(OH)1−yFy/NiO/NF (68 mV dec−1), RuO2/NF (70 mV dec−1) and NiOOH/NiO/NF (75 mV dec−1).

Additionally, the superior OER kinetics of NiFe0.05–N–CP is also reflected by the lowest Tafel slope (76 mV dec−1; Fig. 3b), which is much lower than those of Ni–N–CP (107 mV dec−1) and Fe–N–CP (206 mV dec−1). In addition, the enhanced OER kinetics of NiFex–N–CP is further demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Fig. 3c); the EIS spectra are analyzed using a complex nonlinear least-squares fitting method based on an equivalent circuit (top inset, Fig. 3c). NiFe0.05–N–CP has the lowest Rct value (16.81 Ω) at the open-circuit voltage, which is much lower than that of Ni–N–CP (154.1 Ω). This is due to a charge transfer activation effect from Fe doping in small quantities. In addition, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) associated with the electrochemical surface areas (ECSA) of NiFe0.05–N–CP (0.48 mF cm−2) implied that NiFe0.05–N–CP has the largest ECSA (Fig. S9, ESI†). Therefore, the suitable Fe dopant means that NiFe0.05–N–CP can both have higher conductivity and maintain its rough surface to expose more active sites, which makes NiFe0.05–N–CP locate at the top of the volcano-like OER activity trend among all products (Fig. S10, ESI†).

The OER performances of NiFe0.1O and commercial RuO2 are investigated using a standard three-electrode system with 1.0 M KOH as the electrolyte. Due to the influence of ohmic resistance, the measured reaction currents did not directly reflect the intrinsic properties of the catalysts. We performed iR corrections on all raw data for further analysis. As shown in Fig. 4a, the as-prepared NiFe0.1O shows the lowest overpotential of 274 mV at 10 mA cm−2, compared with NiO (331 mV) and commercial RuO2 (319 mV). This activity improvement is attributed to the optimized structure and grain boundary defects after the incorporation of Fe. To obtain more information about the kinetics of the OER, Tafel slopes were analyzed (Fig. 4b). The Tafel plot of NiFe0.1O shows a much smaller Tafel slope (79.1 mV dec−1) than those of NiO (127.9 mV dec−1) and commercial RuO2 (180.2 mV dec−1), indicating an accelerated reaction kinetics due to the accelerated mass and electron transfer capacity derived from Fe and more active sites exposed by grain boundary defects. It is well known that different Tafel slopes represent different rate determination steps within a given path. The highly charged Fe sites in Fe-doped NiO will increase the oxidation state and facilitate the formation of HO* and O* intermediates. The decreased Tafel slope of NiFe0.1O suggests a more favorable OER activity. Apart from activity, stability is another key parameter for evaluating the performance of OER electrocatalysts. The polarization curve of NiFe0.1O suggests a slight difference after 2000 CV cycles, proving its excellent stability (Fig. 4c). Meanwhile, the chronopotentiometry of NiFe0.1O indicates its long-term stability at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The current density is fluctuant due to the O2 bubbles and has not changed substantially after 10 hours of testing (Fig. 4d). Then, by comparing the TEM images before and after the OER test (Fig. S5†), we found that the layered stacked structure of the sample remained basically unchanged, indicating that the sample was stable.

CoxN-based materials are promising for catalyzing the OER. The construction of the heterojunction can enhance the activity benefiting the optimized electronic structure at the interface. Thus, the 2D Mo2N–CoxN-5 heterojunction is expected to be active for the OER. As shown in Fig. 6a and S27,† the 2D Mo2N–CoxN-5 catalyst only requires an overpotential of 247 mV to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The overpotentials of CoxN, nickel foam, MoxN, RuO2, Mo2N–CoxN-4 and Mo2N–CoxN-6 are 285, 420, 300, 319, 271 and 310 mV, respectively (Fig. 6b and Table S4†). Fig. S28† displays the LSV curves of the catalysts with 90% IR compensation. In this case, the Mo2N–CoxN-5 catalyst requires low overpotentials of 231 and 323 mV to achieve current densities of 10 and 100 mA cm−2, respectively. It is obvious that the 2D porous Mo2N–CoxN-5 sheet shows the best activity among the samples under different conditions. The improved OER activity of the Mo2N–CoxN-5 sheets should be ascribed to the appropriate nitridation degree and grain size. Also, the catalytic performance of the 2D Mo2N–CoxN-5 sheet is governed by the ratio of Mo2N and CoxN. The overpotential of 2D porous Mo2N–CoxN-5 is also lower than those of Mo2N–CoxN-5-0.5 (265 mV), Mo2N–CoxN-5-2 (280 mV) and Mo2N–CoxN-5-5 (310 mV) (Fig. S29a†).

The low ratio of Mo and Co sources can result in the formation of a smaller interface, thus affecting the catalytic activity. On the other hand, the high ratio of Mo/Co can facilitate greater coverage of the CoxN substrate, which is also not favorable for enhancing the activity. The performance of 2D porous Mo2N–CoxN-5 is also superior to that of most reported non-noble metal OER catalysts, such as Co4N (330 mV), Co3O4/N–GN (310 mV), CoOx/FeOx (308 mV) and MoO3/Ni–NiO (320 mV) (Table S5†). Fig. 6c and S29b† show the Tafel slope of all of our catalysts for the OER. The Tafel slope of 2D porous Mo2N–CoxN-5 is 105 mV dec−1, which is lower than those of CoxN (170 mV dec−1), Mo2N–CoxN-4 (131 mV dec−1), Mo2N–CoxN-6 (181 mV dec−1) and other catalysts, demonstrating the faster kinetics of Mo2N–CoxN-5 for water oxidation. As displayed in Fig. 6d, the Cdl values of Mo2N–CoxN-5, CoxN, Mo2N–CoxN-4 and Mo2N–CoxN-6 are 76.76, 51.57, 71.75 and 46.94 mF cm−2, respectively. Obviously, 2D porous Mo2N–CoxN-5 has the largest Cdl value among all of our catalysts (Fig. S30 and S31†). The larger Cdl values and the higher current density from the ECSA-normalized LSV (Fig. S32†) suggest that the Mo2N–CoxN-5 catalyst has an obvious advantage in terms of active surface area and active sites over other catalysts. The Nyquist diagrams shown in Fig. 6e further prove the good ability for the transmission of electrons. Furthermore, the catalyst also possesses good stability for the OER as shown in Fig. 6f, like that for the HER. Typically, the LSV curve after 2000 CV cycles almost completely overlaps with the initial one, and the I–t curve shows no obvious change after the 48 h OER test.

Transition metal nitrides (TMNs) with a noble metal-like electronic configuration around the Fermi level are a research hotspot in the catalysis field. TMNs can trap and activate protons and H2O, thus being promising alternatives to noble metals for water splitting. Many studies have been conducted on early TMNs (ETMNs) and late TMNs (LTMNs). Generally, ETMNs, such as Mo2N and WN, show good HER activity but poor OER activity because the corresponding oxides formed during the reaction are not very active for the OER. Notably, the oxides formed on LTMNs during the reaction are active for the OER. The synergy of LTMNs with the oxide layer endows the materials with good activity for the OER. Xie et al. have reported the synthesis of Co4N porous nanowire array/CC with an overpotential of 257 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (η 10) for the OER. Yu et al. have shown 1D porous cobalt nitride on Ni foam with an η 10 of 95 mV for the HER in KOH medium. Generally, LTMNs, for example Co4N, usually show good activity for one half reaction (OER) at the expense of the activity for the other half-reaction (HER), thus giving a moderate performance for overall water splitting.

The OER catalytic activity of NiFe alloy catalysts, including NiFe and NiFeV LDHs, NiFeP and NiFeVP, was studied under 1 M KOH conditions (Fig. 6a and S10†). NiFeVP also performed optimization tests for the Fe : V ratio and 1 : 1 showed the highest activity compared to other products (Fig. S11a and b†). Due to the high redox peak of NiFe-based OER catalysts, overpotential was measured using chronoamperometry. This NiFeVP showed the best OER activity with η 10 of 234 mV without IR correction, followed by NiFeP (η 10 = 249 mV), NiFeV (η 10 = 276 mV) and NiFe (η 10 = 295 mV) in that order (Fig. 6b), which were much lower than Ir (η 10 = 340 mV). Even at a high current density of 100 mA cm−2, NiFeVP exhibited the lowest overpotential of 360 mV compared to NiFeP (η 100 = 390 mV), NiFeV (η 100 = 3950 mV) and NiFe (η 100 = 430 mV). According to Tafel plot analysis, NiFeVP has the smallest Tafel slope of 34.4 mV dec−1, indicating the fastest OER dynamics compared to NiFeV (37.9 mV dec−1), NiFeP (38.5 mV dec−1), and NiFe (45.9 mV dec−1) (Fig. 6c).

CV and chronopotentiometry analyses were used to evaluate the OER stability of the NiFeVP catalyst (Fig. 6f). Similar to NiCoVP, the activity of NiFeVP improved during the first 150 cycles (Fig. S11c†). However, the reason for this initial activation process is different from that of NiCoVP. During activation cycles, the redox peak at 1.45 V shifts to a higher voltage, and at the same time, the redox transition area increases, indicating the conversion of the surface structure transition from phosphide to hydroxides. In addition, P 2p XPS results show that the metal-phosphide (M-P) peak at 129.5 eV disappeared after the OER, which means that the surface phosphide was oxidized to phosphate ions (Fig. S12†). After this activation process, the CV graphs barely changed up to 1000 cycles. The chronopotentiometry test was also conducted in two different current densities of 10 and 100 mA cm−2 for 30 h continuously. The results in Fig. 6f indicate high durable OER performances of NiFeVP.

Apart from the OER activity, the HER activity of the NiFe LDH/NiCoP@NC electrode was also investigated in 1 M KOH. As shown in the Fig. S8,† the NiFe LDH/NiCoP@NC electrode also exhibited a certain HER catalytic activity with a low overpotential of 127 mV to drive a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which was obviously superior to that of NiFe LDH (208 mV), although a little worse than NiCoP@NC (115 mV).

Herein, hierarchical porous N-doped amorphous carbon-coated NiCoP in situ grown on nickel foam (denoted as NiCoP@NC/NF) was first prepared by step calcination and phosphating of the metal–organic frameworks (MOF, ZIF-67) in situ grown on nickel foam (NF), and then amorphous NiFe LDH nanosheets were directly electrodeposited on the surface of the hierarchical porous NiCoP@NC/NF to form a 3D nano framework (Scheme 1). It is well known that cobalt MOFs (Co-MOFs) are fascinating precursors for preparing numerous hierarchical porous Co-containing materials or Co-based compound@doped-nitrogen carbon composites and have been extensively used in electrocatalysis. In particular, ZIF-67, as one of the most investigated Co-MOFs, was thus selected as the precursor to prepare NiCoP@NC. Owing to the excellent electrical conductivity, rough surface and abundant exposed active sites, fast electron and mass transfer rates, and the cooperating effect, the well-designed 3D NiFe LDH (NiFe LDH/NiCoP@NC/NF) would achieve the desirable OER performance. As a result, the integrated electrocatalysts required an overpotential of only 210 mV to deliver 10 mA cm−2 for OER, and had an ultra-small Tafel slope (35 mV dec−1) as well as outstanding stability. When NiFe LDH/NiCoP@NC/NF was combined with NiCoP@NC/NF as a dual-electrode system for overall water-splitting, only 1.54 V was required to obtain a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 1 M KOH.

Double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was used to further evaluate the OER catalytic activity of CoS1.097/NGF-(650–950). The CV curves at different scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV s−1) were recorded and the capacitor current was collected at 0.218 V (Fig. 5h and S10, ESI†). Based on the CV curves of CoS1.097/NGF-(650–950), the corresponding Cdls were calculated to be 6.7, 82.7, 44.4 and 31.9 mF cm−2 from the slope of the linear fit, respectively (Fig. 5i).
