Heterostructures with elaborately engineered interface provides opportunities to develop higher-performance electrocatalysts for water-splitting.[-] Graphdiyne (GDY), a novel sp-/sp2-cohybridized 2D carbon material, has many unique properties superior to traditional carbon materials including abundant carbon chemical bonds, natural pores, natural band gap, excellent conductivity, and high electrochemical stability, and has been regarded as a rapidly rising star in many fields including electrocatalysis, energy conversion and storage, photoelectricity, etc.[, , , -] Remarkably, the unevenly distributed surface charge in GDY can form infinite active sites, and the sp-hybridized carbon atoms in GDY are conducive to stabilizing 3d TMs by the formation of strong chemical bonds resulting in improved charge transport ability between TM and GDY, and thus enhancing the intrinsic activity of each active site.[, ] In addition, GDY can be grown on arbitrary substrates allowing for the formation of optimal interfaces with maximized number of active sites, and improved charge transfer ability, catalytic activity, and stability in electrocatalysis. Moreover, GDY has been demonstrated to be the most stable carbon allotrope. These excellent properties of GDY provide a broad space for us to design high-performance electrocatalysts.

X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), density functional theory (DFT) calculation, and Raman spectroscopy measurements were further performed to gain a deep understanding into the chemical structure and composition of the catalysts. As shown in Figure 4a, the peaks located at 26.6° and 42.5° correspond to (002) and (101) planes of CC. All the diffraction peaks of the MnCo2O4 sample are well indexed to the typical spinel MnCo2O4 (JCPDS No. 23–1237, space group: Fd3m). The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 18.3°, 30.6°, 36.2°, 44.1°, 54.8°, 58.6°, and 64.4° correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes of cubic MnCo2O4, respectively. It shows that no other impurity peaks are observed from the XRD pattern, indicating the MnCoCH precursor completely transform to the MnCo2O4 nanowire arrays. The XPS survey spectrum for NW-MnCo2O4/GDY shows the presence of Mn, Co, O, and C elements in the nanowires (Figure 4) in accordance with the elemental mapping results (Figure 2j–2n). The C 1s XPS spectra of the NW-MnCo2O4/GDY displays five sub-peaks at 284.1 eV (sp2-C), 284.5 eV (sp-C), 285.7 eV (CO), 288.0 eV (CO), and 290.2 eV (π-π* transition), respectively (Figure 4b). Compared with pure GDY, the emergence π-π* transition peak at 290.2 eV implies the restoration of the delocalized π conjugation, which reveals the interaction between MnCo2O4 and GDY. The Co 2p XPS spectra (Figure 4c) show two spin-orbit doublets of Co3+ (780.1 eV for Co 2p3/2; 795.1 eV for Co 2p1/2) and Co2+ (781.9 eV for Co 2p3/2; 796.9 eV for Co 2p1/2). Compared with pristine NW-MnCo2O4, the Co 2p XPS spectrum exhibits a significant negative in binding energies, revealing the charge transfer from GDY to MnCo2O4. Similarly, Mn 2p XPS spectra (Figure 4d) confirm the presence of Mn2+ (641.9 and 652.9 eV) and Mn3+ (642.9 and 654.4 eV).

Compared with pure NW-MnCo2O4, the Mn 2p XPS spectra for NW-MnCo2O4/GDY shift to lower binding energies. These results imply the existence of mixed valence states of Mn species and obvious charge transfer between GDY and MnCo2O4. Besides, DFT calculations reveal that the acetylenic C atoms can interact with Mn atoms (the purple balls) or O atoms (the red balls), while the Co atoms (the blue balls) are mainly exposed in the constructed model (Figure 4e). An obvious charge transfer between MnCo2O4 and GDY (Figure 4f), which mainly occurs at the interface sites adjacent to the acetylenic chains in MnCo2O4/GDY, can also be observed. These findings all could effectively improve the catalytic performances. [, ] Figure 4g,h shows the Raman spectra of the samples. The pure GDY samples present four prominent peaks at around 1373, 1581, 1923, and 2177 cm−1, corresponding to the D band, the G band, and the vibration of diyne linkage in GDY structure, respectively (Figure 4g). In the Raman spectra of the NW-MnCo2O4@GDY, the peaks at about 183, 473, 514, and 675 cm−1 are assigned to the modes of MnCo2O4 and three peaks at about 1364 (D band), 1573 (G band), and 2082 cm−1 (vibrations of diyne links) corresponding to the GDY species (Figure 4h). These results evidence the successful growth of GDY on NW-MnCo2O4. In addition, Raman measurements can further provide evidence for the formation of new defects in samples by quantifying the ID/IG. The NW-MnCo2O4/GDY shows a larger ID/IG ratio (0.80) than pure GDY (0.67) indicates that the generation of new defects after the incorporation of GDY with NW-MnCo2O4, which benefits the improvement of catalytic activity.

XPS was used to assess the near-surface composition and speciation of Co and F species as a function of the annealing temperature (Figure 3i). After drying at 150 °C, a near-surface F/Co atomic ratio of 2.89 was determined, which is lower than the experimental bulk ratio (4.48, Table S1, Supporting Information) and that expected from the stoichiometry of the MOF structure (4.00), indicating a certain surface enrichment in cobalt. For this material, a Co2p binding energy (BE) of 783.9 eV and the detection of the characteristic shake-up satellite features agree with the existence of Co2+-carboxylates in the structure of the CoTFBDC coordination polymer. The corresponding F1s spectrum showed a major component at a BE of 690.2 eV, which was also the only F1s band detected for the corresponding unsupported CoTFBDC MOF (peaking at 690.1 eV, Figure S11, Supporting Information). This BE is characteristic of polyfluorinated benzene rings and can thus be ascribed to the fluorinated organic linker in the MOF structure. Next to this major component, two lower BE contributions were additionally detected in the composite. The band at 689.1 eV falls in the range of electron-rich covalent C–F species and can thus arise from an electronic interaction of a fraction of the fluorinated linkers in the 2D MOF crystals with the electron-donating graphitic support. The second contribution peaking at 687.8 eV has been reported for fluorinated graphene and suggests that, already at mild temperatures (150 °C), part of the fluorine is emitted from the MOF, migrates by spillover and reacts with the graphitic support. The O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two components. The major one at 534.4 eV was detected as a single contribution in the unsupported MOF (Figure S10, Supporting Information) and is thus ascribed to the carboxylate moieties in the linker. The lower BE contribution at 532.1 eV arises from oxygen groups on the graphitic carrier.

Herein we report the synthesis of metal/carbon electrocatalysts via the assembly and decomposition of composite precursors consisting of intercalated 2D cobalt-based MOF crystals and graphitic lamellae. On the one hand, the compositionally regular MOF crystals represent a suitable shuttle to introduce finely dispersed electrocatalytically active metal species into the graphitic matrix, while controlling their direct chemical vicinity via judicious choice of the organic linker in the starting macromolecular precursor. While highly active OER electrocatalysts based on single 2D nanomaterials such as layered double hydroxides (LDHs) or heteroatom-doped graphene have been described in the recent literature, here we integrate two different 2D nanomaterials into layered composites to showcase nanospatial control over the electrocatalytic promotion effect, bringing a highly electronegative promoter such as fluorine in atomic intimacy with electroactive cobalt species. On the other hand, the graphitic matrix confers remarkable electrical conductivity. The layered morphology of both components in the precursor composite facilitates their integration and improves their mutual contact area thus maintaining a tight contact between the electrically isolating active phase and the highly conductive graphitic current collector in the ultimate electrocatalyst material. This route has been exploited to synthesize noble metal-free electrocatalysts with unprecedented performance for the OER in alkaline media.

MOF and exfoliated graphite (EG) 2D components were integrated to form a heterostructure by co-dispersion in N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) followed by slow and complete solvent removal. Fourier-Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) provided evidences for the intercalation of MOF nanocrystals within the EG sheets, which hamper their re-stacking upon drying (Figure S4, Supporting Information). Moreover, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) showed increased electron spin relaxation rates for Co2+ ions in the MOF structure upon incorporation into the composites, as a result of an enhanced electron mobility, indicative of an effective electronic contact between the MOF nanocrystals and the conductive EG support material (Figure S5, Supporting Information). SEM imaging was used on focused-ion-beam (FIB) milled cross-sections of consolidated pellets in order to assess the mesoscale spatial distribution of the two components in the MOF/EG composite materials. As shown in Figure 1f–h, the stacked architecture of the composite was revealed, displaying a highly folded external surface with slit-type pores, for reactant transport, as a result of the stacking of graphite nanosheets. In addition, cross-sectional imaging showed the MOF nanosheet crystals to be uniformly distributed within the pellet at a mesoscopic–micrometer length scale and well integrated with the exfoliated graphite, without obvious gap spaces between the two materials. High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were applied to investigate the integration of the two materials at the nanoscale. In this case, the mesoscale architecture of the composite was disintegrated by ultrasonication to achieve nanometer thin specimens, thus sufficiently transparent to the electron beam. As illustrated in Figure 1j–n and Figure S6 (Supporting Information), the resulting MOF/EG composites consist of flakes of EG, with micrometer lateral dimensions, decorated with smaller MOF nanolamellae, identified by their high local concentrations of Co and F. Overall, these results confirmed the successful intercalation of both 2D nanomaterials into an heterostructure, as schematically illustrated in Figure 1i, thus attaining a high contact between the macromolecular metal precursor and the graphitic current collector in the composites.

The DFT calculation was performed on the VASP code. [Co0.6Ni0.4(CO3)0.5(OH)]24 and [Cu(OH)2]8 supercells were constructed according to the previously reported structures. To reveal the synergistic impact of Co and Ni on the electrocatalytic performance of the CoNiCH, [Ni(CO3)0.5(OH)]24, and [Co(CO3)0.5(OH)]24 supercells were also constructed to perform the calculation. Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation functional was employed to describe electron–orbit interaction energy. The cutoff energy was set to 500 eV, and k-point sampling was selected in the first Brillouin zone with an energy tolerance of 10−5 eV per atom. During the geometric optimization, the lattice constants and atomic positions were relaxed, and the electronic minimization accuracy was 1 × 10−5 eV. The LDA + U correction calculation was used for Ni, Co, and Cu atoms, in which U = 4.216 eV and J = 0.816 eV for Ni atoms, U = 4.216 eV and J = 0.8024 eV for Co atoms, U = 4.080 eV and J = 0.789 eV for Cu atoms, respectively.
